text: Ukrainian opposition leader Viktor Yushchenko has warned foreign nations against taking sides in the country's new presidential runoff election.Mr. Yushchenko said outsiders should only work to ensure the vote is fair.His comments followed pledges from the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe to support Ukraine's new court-ordered presidential run-off election.Thousands of Mr. Yushchenko's supporters have continued their vigil in the capital Kiev, where Ukraine's parliament remained deadlocked on proposals to amend the country's election laws.Opposition lawmakers support legal changes aimed at preventing voter fraud. Pro-government politicians are pushing for constitutional changes that would reduce the powers of the president.On Saturday, Ukraine's Supreme Court threw out results from the last presidential election and scheduled a new run-off for December 26.Some information for this report provided by AP.

Activists have reported that Greek authorities have intercepted a ship that was attempting to head to Gaza, in a bid to break the Israeli blockade of the Palestinian territory. The ship, named "Freedom Flotilla", was carrying humanitarian aid and activists from various countries who were hoping to deliver much-needed supplies to the people of Gaza.The activists have criticized the Greek government for stopping the ship, arguing that the blockade imposed by Israel is illegal under international law and that the people of Gaza are in desperate need of assistance. They have called on the international community to put pressure on both Greece and Israel to allow the ship to continue its journey to Gaza.The Greek government has not yet commented on the incident. Israel maintains that the blockade is necessary for security reasons, as it seeks to prevent weapons and other materials that could be used for military purposes from entering Gaza.The interception of the ship has sparked outrage among supporters of the Palestinian cause, who see it as yet another example of the ongoing oppression faced by the people of Gaza. The activists on board the ship have vowed to continue their efforts to break the blockade and deliver aid to Gaza, despite the obstacles they face.

New research has revealed a concerning decline in shark populations off the coast of Australia. The study, conducted by marine biologists at the Australian Marine Conservation Society, found that shark numbers have plummeted by 30% in the past decade.The decline in shark numbers is attributed to a combination of factors, including overfishing, habitat destruction, and climate change. Sharks play a crucial role in maintaining the health of marine ecosystems, and their dwindling numbers could have far-reaching consequences for the ocean's biodiversity.Conservationists are calling for urgent action to protect Australia's shark populations. Measures such as stronger regulations on fishing practices, the establishment of marine protected areas, and increased public awareness about the importance of sharks in the ecosystem are being proposed to reverse the decline in shark numbers.The findings of the study serve as a stark reminder of the urgent need to protect Australia's marine life and highlight the critical role that sharks play in maintaining the health of our oceans.

A tragic incident occurred in Afghanistan today as a US journalist and translator were killed in an attack. The journalist, who was working for a major news organization, was on assignment in the war-torn country when the attack took place.The identities of the victims have not been released pending notification of their families. The US State Department has confirmed the deaths and expressed condolences to the families of the victims.The attack serves as a stark reminder of the dangers faced by journalists and translators working in conflict zones around the world. The freedom of the press is essential for a functioning democracy, and the loss of these brave individuals is a blow to the journalism community.Investigations into the attack are ongoing, and authorities are working to determine the motives behind the targeted killing. The safety of journalists and translators working in dangerous environments must be a top priority for all involved.Our thoughts are with the families and loved ones of the journalist and translator who lost their lives in this senseless act of violence. May they rest in peace.

Tens of thousands of people have marched in northern Spain for a third consecutive day to protest the acquittal of five men on gang rape charges.Local police in Pamplona estimated the size of the crowd at Saturday’s march at 35,000.An 18-year-old woman was attacked during the city’s famed San Fermin bull-running festival in 2016.The five men, whose members named their WhatsApp group “The Pack,” were convicted Thursday on a lesser felony of sexual abuse and sentenced to nine years each in prison.

The leader of Egypt's Muslim Brotherhood and nearly 700 members of the group went on trial Tuesday on charges including murder and inciting violence. The trial of Mohamed Badie, the Muslim Brotherhood’s spiritual guide during his arrest last year, and the others comes a day after the same court in Minya sentenced 529 Brotherhood members to death. They had been charged with murdering a police officer, attacking a police station and other acts of violence. The trial of Badie and the others was adjourned until April 28, when judgment and sentencing is expected, a lawyer said. Defense lawyers boycotted Tuesday's session of the trial to protest the mass death sentences issued Monday by the same court in the city of Minya. “We believe the trial's fairness, which is a right enshrined in the criminal code, was absent in this case, and therefore we take this stand, not in favor of any party. We are all aware and in agreement about the crimes committed by the Muslim Brotherhood," lawyer Tarek Fouda, head of Minya's Lawyer's Syndicate said. "However, we must defend the rule of law in Egypt and ensure justice for all.” That first trial took just two days, drawing international criticism over whether the proceedings were fair.

The economy, Iraq, and health care continue to be prominent issues in the United States as the country grapples with various challenges.In terms of the economy, the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic has led to widespread job losses and economic uncertainty.

North Korea is now threatening to withdraw from next month's planned summit between leader Kim Jong Un and U.S. President Donald Trump.In a statement released Wednesday morning through the North's official KCNA news agency, first Vice Foreign Minister Kim Kye Gwan says Pyongyang will have to "reconsider" whether to take part in the June 12 meeting in Singapore if the United States continues to demand that the regime unilaterally abandon its nuclear weapons arsenal.Kim said North Korea will not be interested in the summit if Washington tries "to push us unilaterally into a corner and force us to give up nukes."The statement is the latest move by North Korea in just the last 24 hours that appears to scuttle a new period of detente on the Korean peninsula. High-level talks scheduled for Wednesday with the South were abruptly cancelled by Pyongyang, citing the current joint U.S.-South Korean military exercises. KCNA said the Max Thunder exercises are a "rehearsal for invasion" of North Korea and a provocation.The statement also warned that the U.S. "will also have to undertake careful deliberations about the fate of the planned North Korea-U.S. summit in light of this provocative military ruckus jointly conducted with the South Korean authorities."The White House has said very little except to say it will continue to coordinate closely with its allies.The Pentagon describes the Max Thunder exercises as a routine annual training to "enhance the ROK-U.S. alliance's ability to defend the ROK (South Korea) and enhance interoperability and readiness." It says this has been clear for many decades.State Department spokeswoman Heather Nauert pointed out that Kim has said he understands and appreciates the importance of such exercises to the United States.

Despite being one of the oldest and most marginalized minority groups in the United States, Native Americans have been largely overlooked in the current presidential campaign. With only a few mentions and little substantive policy discussion, many Indigenous peoples feel that their voices and issues are not being given the attention they deserve.According to the US Census Bureau, Native Americans make up just over 2% of the country's population, yet they face significant challenges in terms of poverty, healthcare, education, and other issues. Many Native American tribes also continue to fight for land rights and recognition of their sovereignty.While some candidates have made efforts to reach out to Native American communities, their efforts have been largely symbolic and lacking in concrete proposals. The lack of attention to Native American issues in the campaign is particularly troubling given the history of colonization and oppression that Indigenous peoples have faced in the US.As the presidential campaign heats up, it is crucial for candidates to address the concerns of all Americans, including Native Americans. By listening to their voices and advocating for policies that address their unique needs, candidates can demonstrate a commitment to equality and justice for all. It is time for Native Americans to be seen, heard, and included in the political conversation.

A group of dedicated mentors are making a significant impact on the lives of homeless children in our community. These mentors are providing much-needed guidance, support, and encouragement to help these children navigate the challenges they face on a daily basis.Through one-on-one mentoring sessions, the children are able to develop important life skills, build their self-esteem, and set goals for their future. The mentors also act as positive role models, showing the children what is possible when they work hard and believe in themselves.One of the mentors, Sarah Thompson, shared her experience working with the homeless children. She stated, "It is truly rewarding to see the progress these children make when they have someone there to support them and believe in them. I have seen their confidence grow, their grades improve, and their outlook on life become more positive."The impact of these mentors goes beyond just academics. They also provide emotional support and a sense of stability for these children who may not have a stable living situation. By forming a strong bond with their mentees, the mentors are able to create a safe and nurturing environment for the children to thrive.Overall, the efforts of these mentors are making a difference in the lives of homeless children, giving them hope for a brighter future. Their dedication and commitment to helping these children succeed is truly commendable and serves as a reminder of the power of mentorship in changing lives for the better.

British Prime Minister Theresa May goes before European Union leaders Wednesday as the two sides faced stalled talks in the negotiations over Britain's exit from the bloc.Months of talks have produced agreements on many parts of how to unravel the relationship, but some key hurdles remain, including how to handle the border between Northern Ireland and EU member Ireland.After May addresses the group, the other 27 leaders will have dinner on their own to discuss the Brexit negotiations.EU Council President Donald Tusk said ahead of Wednesday's meetings he planned to ask May "whether she has concrete proposals on how to break the impasse."Tusk said that will determine if it is possible to resolve the differences and proceed toward the orderly breakup planned for March of next year.He also warned that EU leaders need to discuss how to prepare for a so-called "no-deal Brexit," under which Britain would exit the EU without any formal measures in place resolving issues such as trade and freedom of movement.British Prime Minister Theresa May makes a statement on Brexit negotiations with the European Union, at 10 Downing Street, in London, Sept. 21, 2018.?EU leaders will decide whether to hold a special summit in November, but Tusk said there needs to be reason to believe the two sides are close to a breakthrough.The European Union wants Northern Ireland, regardless of whether there’s a subsequent trade deal with Britain, to remain inside the bloc’s customs union so as to avoid the necessity of border controls between the British province and the Irish Republic.

A recent meeting of Ugandans residing in North America focused on the importance of development and economic opportunity in their home country. The gathering, which took place in a major city in the United States, brought together individuals from various professional backgrounds and regions of Uganda.During the meeting, attendees discussed ways to support and contribute to the development of Uganda, including through investment opportunities, skills training programs, and partnerships with local businesses and organizations. They emphasized the need for sustainable development that benefits all Ugandans and promotes economic growth and prosperity.Several speakers shared their own success stories and experiences in the business world, inspiring others to pursue similar opportunities. They also highlighted the potential for collaboration between Ugandans living abroad and those in Uganda to create a more vibrant and competitive economy.Overall, the meeting served as a platform for Ugandans in North America to come together, share ideas, and work towards a common goal of contributing to the development and economic prosperity of their homeland.Participants left the meeting feeling motivated and empowered to make a positive impact on Uganda's future.

TAIPEI, TAIWAN - The United States’ tacit acceptance of Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte’s deadly campaign against illegal drugs is expected to put the worst in bilateral relations behind them and lead to renewed cooperation between the two longtime allies despite a wary China.U.S. Deputy Assistant Secretary of State James Walsh said in a telephone news briefing Jan. 31 he was “cautiously optimistic” about how the Philippines is ridding itself of illegal drugs and that Washington would support the campaign as long as it was done legally.Activists believe the Duterte government has let police kill as many as 7,000 people, without trials, since taking office in June 2016.Walsh’s comments “hint of a growing appreciation of the positive impact of the administration’s anti-illegal drug campaign,” the Philippine presidential website said.That exchange, following an upbeat meeting between Duterte and U.S. President Donald Trump in November, begins to push back a storm of comments that the rough-spoken Philippine leader has made against the United States largely over its criticism of the anti-drug campaign.Duterte paired his remarks with the establishment of friendly ties with U.S. Cold War rival China.“You could just say that probably (the relationship) has sort of stabilized,” said Jay Batongbacal, a University of the Philippines law and international maritime affairs professor.FILE - People take part in a protest against the government of Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte in front of the Philippine consulate in New York, Dec. 10, 2017.After Duterte took office on popular pledges to stop sales of illegal drugs, human rights groups accused him of letting police shoot to kill suspects without trial. His approval ratings grew at home as Filipinos felt crime was easing, but the U.S. government under then President Barack Obama criticized the suspected extrajudicial killings.Washington withheld millions of dollars in funding for the Philippine National Police after pledging $32 million for Philippines law enforcement.Duterte once used profanity to describe Obama and asked the United States to leave the Philippines.

Ethiopia's finance minister says western donors' freezing of $375 million in aid to his country is unacceptable.Sofian Ahmed said Friday the direct aid amounts to 10 percent of Ethiopia's annual budget.Western diplomats said Thursday the European Union, the World Bank and other donors would withhold the aid as a result of Ethiopia's crackdown on opposition leaders and journalists.Media reports say the donors hope to disburse the money through other channels to address Ethiopia's widespread poverty.The government crackdown followed clashes between security forces and protesters last month, in which at least 46 people were killed. Wednesday, a judge ordered the continued confinement of more than 80 opposition leaders and journalists detained during the protests.A total of 131 people are charged with plotting against the Ethiopian government, treason, and genocide.

A doctor in eastern Congo has contracted Ebola, marking a worrisome development in the ongoing outbreak in the region, according to the World Health Organization (WHO). This incident represents a "dreaded" scenario, as health workers are at high risk of contracting the virus while treating patients.The doctor, who has not been named, is receiving care and treatment in Butembo, a city in North Kivu province where the outbreak has been concentrated. It is believed that the doctor was exposed to the virus while caring for a patient at a hospital in the area.This new case highlights the challenges that health workers face in containing the Ebola outbreak, which has been ongoing in eastern Congo since August 2018. The region has faced numerous obstacles in controlling the spread of the virus, including ongoing conflict and community mistrust of healthcare workers.The WHO has called for increased support and resources to help contain the outbreak and protect healthcare workers on the frontlines. The organization has also emphasized the importance of community engagement and education in preventing further spread of the virus.This latest development serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing threat posed by Ebola in eastern Congo, and the need for continued vigilance and collaboration in the fight against the virus.

A recent poll has revealed a growing dissatisfaction among Americans with the current system of government in the United States. The survey, conducted by a leading research firm, found that a significant majority of respondents feel that the current system is in need of major reforms.According to the poll results, many Americans feel that the current political system is plagued by corruption, gridlock, and inefficiency. There is a widespread belief that elected officials are more concerned with serving their own interests rather than the needs of the people they are supposed to represent.Furthermore, the poll found that a large number of Americans feel that the current two-party system is failing to adequately represent the diversity of opinions and beliefs within the country. Many feel that third-party candidates are unfairly marginalized and that the current system stifles true democracy.The poll results also show a strong desire among Americans for changes to the way elections are conducted, with many expressing support for measures such as campaign finance reform, term limits for elected officials, and the abolition of the Electoral College.Overall, the poll findings suggest that there is a hunger for change among the American public when it comes to the current system of government. It remains to be seen whether these sentiments will translate into concrete action and reforms in the future.

The White House has revealed that some Bush administration staffers may have used their private Republican Party e-mail accounts to conduct government business, and it says those messages may have been improperly deleted.Congressional Democrats have been seeking the e-mails as part of their inquiry into the dismissals of eight federal prosecutors. White House spokesman Scott Stanzel says that some of those lost e-mails may have discussed the firings.Dozens of administration aides, including chief political advisor Karl Rove, had e-mail addresses set up through the Republican Party to use strictly for political work.Federal law prohibits government employees from engaging in political business using government-owned property.

(Səsli faylı yükləmək üçün bu linkin üzərində saö düyməyə basıb, menyudan "Yadda saxla"nı seçin: MP3-ü yükləyin)A mentor is someone who teaches or advises a younger person with less experience. January is National Mentoring Month in the United States. Some mentoring programs connect adults with children who need help. Researchers say mentoring can reduce drug and alcohol use, and violence among young people. In Southern California, a nonprofit group called School on Wheels has volunteers who tutor children from homeless families. Experts estimate that one in every 50 children in the country is homeless at some time during the year. They might live in shelters or weekly hotels or abandoned buildings. They might live on the streets. They might live anywhere but a permanent home. Sinead Chilton is a marketing consultant for School on Wheels. She moved from London to the United States. She says she was surprised to learn how big the problem was. “When I first moved here ten years ago and I heard about School on Wheels, I didn’t even consider the fact that there were homeless children in America. And then when I realized that there were 1.6 million homeless children in America, it just floored me.” Ms. Chilton has worked with School on Wheels for more than six years. She still remembers the first student she tutored. She met with her every week at a local church. The church gave shelter to people on winter nights. The girl slept on the floor. “It just struck me as just so devastating and just how can you focus on school and how can you even think about homework when those are the circumstances that you’re living in?” A retired teacher named Agnes Stevens started School on Wheels eight years ago. She wanted to help homeless children stay in school. The group now has more than 1000 volunteers. Each year they tutor as many as 2000 students.

In a heartwarming display of solidarity and compassion, Americans across the country have rallied behind the Darfur refugee soccer team. The team, made up of talented players who have fled violence and persecution in their home country, has captured the hearts of many with their resilience and determination.Support for the team has been pouring in from various sources, including donations for equipment and uniforms, as well as offers for training facilities and coaching assistance. Many Americans have also expressed their willingness to attend games and cheer on the players as they compete in local leagues.The Darfur refugee soccer team serves as a powerful reminder of the human spirit's ability to persevere in the face of adversity. By coming together to support these players, Americans are not only showing their solidarity with refugees around the world but also celebrating the power of sport to bring communities together.As the team continues to practice and compete, they serve as an inspiration to us all, reminding us of the importance of compassion, empathy, and support for those in need. The outpouring of support for the Darfur refugee soccer team is a testament to the generosity and kindness of the American people.

In a latest development in the ongoing feud between President Donald Trump and Attorney General Jeff Sessions, Trump has escalated the tension by criticizing Sessions on Twitter once again. Trump has been vocal about his disappointment with Sessions for recusing himself from the Russia investigation, calling him "weak" and questioning his loyalty. This public feud has put a strain on the relationship between the President and his top law enforcement official. Despite the pressure from Trump, Sessions has remained steadfast in his role as Attorney General, focusing on carrying out the Department of Justice's agenda. The future of this relationship remains uncertain as Trump continues to express his frustration with Sessions.

The White House has declared its opposition to a bill that would give Washington, D.C. a vote in Congress.A White House spokesman Friday said the Constitution is clear that only residents of states, not districts, can elect people to Congress.The bill now being considered by the House of Representatives would add two votes to the 435-member chamber - one for the nation's capital, which traditionally votes Democratic. The other seat would go to the majority-Republican western state of Utah.Democratic leaders say the proposal is expected to pass when it goes to the House floor, perhaps as early as next week. But analysts say the bill is likely to die in the Senate.The District of Columbia has a non-voting delegate in the House of Representatives.

A recent study has found that young adults in the United States are experiencing downward mobility, with many struggling to achieve the same level of success as their parents.The study, conducted by the Pew Research Center, revealed that only about half of adults aged 25 to 29 earn more than their parents did at the same age. This marks a significant decline from previous generations, where upward mobility was more common.Factors contributing to this trend include rising student loan debt, stagnant wages, and a challenging job market. Many young adults are finding it difficult to secure stable employment that offers opportunities for advancement.Additionally, the cost of living has continued to rise, making it harder for young adults to save money, purchase homes, and build wealth. This has led to a sense of frustration and disillusionment among many in this age group.Experts warn that if this trend continues, it could have long-term implications for the overall economic well-being of the country. They stress the importance of addressing these issues through policy changes and investments in education and job training programs.As young adults grapple with these challenges, it remains to be seen how they will navigate the road to economic stability and prosperity in the years to come.

A devastating earthquake struck Indonesia, leaving at least 75 people dead and trapping thousands more under collapsed buildings. The 6.5 magnitude quake hit the island of Sulawesi, causing widespread destruction in the city of Mamuju. Rescue teams are working tirelessly to save those who are still trapped, while emergency services are struggling to provide aid to the injured. The Indonesian government has declared a state of emergency and called for international assistance to help deal with the aftermath of this tragedy. Our thoughts are with the victims and their families during this difficult time.

In a surprising turn of events, the United States has settled a high-profile money-laundering case linked to Russian lawyer Sergei Magnitsky on the eve of the trial. The case, which involved allegations of a $230 million fraud scheme, was set to go to trial this week.The settlement comes after years of legal battles and investigations into the money-laundering scheme, which was allegedly carried out by Russian officials and businessmen. The case has drawn international attention due to its connection to Magnitsky, who uncovered the fraud scheme before being arrested and dying in a Russian prison in 2009.While the terms of the settlement have not been disclosed, the resolution of the case without a trial has raised questions about the motivations behind the decision. Some speculate that the settlement may have been reached to avoid further scrutiny of the case and potential revelations about the extent of the fraud scheme.The settlement also comes at a time of heightened tensions between the United States and Russia, with ongoing disputes over issues such as election interference, cyberattacks, and human rights violations. The outcome of this case is likely to have implications for future investigations and legal actions related to money-laundering and corruption.

Billionaire philanthropist and Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates has recently endorsed a scathing critique of military intelligence operations in Afghanistan. The report, conducted by a team of experts, highlights significant shortcomings and failures in the intelligence strategies employed by the US military in the region.Gates, known for his work in global health and education through the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, has long been an advocate for efficient and effective use of resources in tackling complex issues. In a statement released this week, Gates emphasized the importance of learning from past mistakes and reassessing strategies in order to improve outcomes in Afghanistan.The critique of military intelligence in Afghanistan comes at a critical time, as the US prepares to withdraw its troops from the country after nearly two decades of conflict. The report suggests that a lack of coordination, outdated technologies, and a failure to adapt to the rapidly changing landscape of warfare have hindered the success of intelligence operations in the region.Gates' endorsement of the critique is seen as a significant development, given his influence and credibility in both the public and private sectors. As the US continues to navigate its role in Afghanistan, the recommendations put forth in the report could have far-reaching implications for future military operations and intelligence strategies in the region.

French President Emmanuel Macron was both humble and resolute as he spoke publicly for the first time on the anti-government protests that have shaken the country."I take my share of responsibility. I might have hurt people with my words," Macron said in a nationwide broadcast speech Monday night.He also said he recognizes that a proposed tax hike on pensions was "unjust."But Macron called the anger that has boiled over in the past weeks the result of what he describes as a 40-year-long "malaise," especially among rural French.The president declared an "economic and social state of emergency."A man with a sign reading "Macron resign" waves a French flag as "yellow vests" (Gilets jaunes) protestors gather to protest against rising oil prices and living costs at the highway's toll of La Barque, Dec. 9, 2018, near Marseille, southern France.Along with cutting the tax on pensioners, there will be a government-funded $113 boost in the monthly minimum wage, taxes on overtime pay will be scrapped, and large businesses have been asked to give workers a tax-free, end-of-the-year bonus.But Macron stood firm against the street protesters, saying there will be "no indulgence" for those who smash windows, loot stores and attack police.

The music charts for the week ending Oct. 31 have been released, showcasing the top 5 songs that have been dominating the airwaves. Taking the number one spot is "Stay" by The Kid LAROI and Justin Bieber. This collaboration between the Australian rapper and Canadian pop star has been a fan favorite since its release, with its catchy melody and emotional lyrics resonating with listeners.Coming in at number two is "Industry Baby" by Lil Nas X featuring Jack Harlow. This upbeat and energetic track has been a huge hit since its debut, with its bold visuals and clever wordplay garnering attention from fans and critics alike.In third place is "Bad Habits" by Ed Sheeran. The British singer-songwriter's latest single has been a chart-topper since its release, with its infectious beat and relatable lyrics making it a standout track."Good 4 U" by Olivia Rodrigo secures the fourth spot on the charts. The breakout star continues to dominate the music scene with her angsty and raw lyrics, capturing the hearts of fans worldwide.Rounding out the top 5 is "Montero (Call Me By Your Name)" by Lil Nas X. This controversial yet empowering anthem has been a standout hit for the artist, showcasing his bold and unapologetic approach to music.These top 5 songs have been making waves in the music industry and are sure to continue to be on repeat for fans around the world. Stay tuned for next week's chart-toppers.

Turkey appears to be taking a different stance than its European and U.S. allies in the wake of Iran's elections. The Turkish president and prime minister were among the first of the few countries to congratulate Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad on his disputed victory. Questions are being raised in Turkey about whether it is becoming out of step with its western allies over Iran. Turkey's U.S. and European allies are voicing increasing criticism of the Iranian presidential election and crackdown by its security forces on demonstrators. But Ankara has remained silent since Turkish President Abdullah Gul sent a congratulatory message to his Iranian counterpart Mahmoud Ahmadinejad. On Monday, Turkish Foreign Minister Ahmed Davutoglu refused to criticize the crackdown.He says Turkey's government believes the problems in Iran will be solved by its inner mechanisms, with the best possible result. He says Turkey hopes Iran's dynamic and well-attended presidential election will not be overshadowed by the recent developments.Working to improve relationsForeign Minster Davutoglu advocates Turkey working to improve its relations with all its neighbors, including Iran. Last year while the rest of the Europe was giving the Iranian president the cold shoulder, President Gul hosted Mr. Ahmadinejad in Istanbul. And despite U.S. criticism, Turkey is increasingly eyeing Iran as an alternative to Russia for its energy supplies. But Turkish journalist Akif Emre says the ruling Islamic AK Party's Iran policy does not mean it is rejecting its Western allies. "Turkey's new policy is to [maintain a] stronger relationship with the West, and also open a new area from Africa to the Middle East and neighbors," Emre said.That policy has seen Turkey's government courting controversial groups like Hamas and Sudan President Omar al-Bashir, who has been indicted for genocide by the International court.Impossible balancing actBut International relations expert Cengiz Aktar of Istanbul's Bachesehir University, says trying to have close ties with Iran and the West is an impossible balancing act."

OSAKA, JAPAN - Amid renewed concern among America’s allies about the U.S. president’s commitment to them, Donald Trump is seeking to offer reassuring words after arriving in Japan for the Group of 20 leaders’ summit.During his first meeting in Osaka -- a working dinner with Australian Prime Minister Scott Morrison -- Trump was asked by a reporter about a perception that an “America alone” approach is adversely affecting some of Washington’s traditional closest allies in the Asia-Pacific region.“We work with our allies. We take care of our allies,” Trump responded while noting he had “inherited massive trade deficits with our allies. And we even help our allies militarily.”During the dinner Trump “stressed his commitment to maximizing the economic partnership with Australia through fair, balanced, and mutually beneficial trade and investment,” according to White House Principal Deputy Press Secretary Hogan Gidley.G-20 host Shinzo Abe, Japan’s prime minister, is hoping for reassuring words to be uttered publicly by Trump when they meet Friday morning in Osaka.In recent days, the president has questioned the fairness of the U.S.-Japan defense alliance.“If Japan is attacked, we will fight World War III. We will go in and protect them with our lives and with our treasure,” Trump said during a telephone interview with Fox Business News on Wednesday. “We will fight at all costs …but if we are attacked, Japan doesn’t have to help us at all. They can watch on a Sony television.”

Israel is seeking to establish "red lines" on Iran's nuclear program, while Palestinians are pushing for a United Nations upgrade. The Israeli government is calling for clear boundaries on Iran's nuclear activities, fearing that the country may be moving closer to developing nuclear weapons. On the other hand, Palestinians are seeking to upgrade their status at the UN from observer to non-member state, a move that could potentially enhance their international recognition and legitimacy. The two issues highlight the ongoing tensions and complex relationships in the Middle East region. Stay tuned for further developments on both fronts.

In a recent statement, a US diplomat has called for new sanctions against Iran. The diplomat cited Iran's continued support for terrorist organizations and development of nuclear weapons as reasons for imposing additional sanctions. The diplomat emphasized the importance of holding Iran accountable for their actions and preventing further destabilization in the region. It remains to be seen whether other countries will support this call for new sanctions against Iran. Iran has not yet responded to these calls for new sanctions. Stay tuned for updates on this developing story.

The United States government has announced a $1 million reward for information leading to the capture of a Romanian fugitive wanted on charges of human trafficking and money laundering. The fugitive, identified as Ionut Moldovan, is believed to have fled to Romania after being indicted in the US for his alleged involvement in a criminal network that exploited vulnerable individuals for profit.According to authorities, Moldovan is considered to be a flight risk and is believed to have connections to organized crime groups in Eastern Europe.

Interpol says inspections of "food" shipments have turned up more than 10,000 tons and a million liters of fake, illicit or toxic food and beverages during a four-month period.Discoveries in 57 countries worldwide included fake Sudanese sugar mixed with fertilizer and 85 tons of Italian olives “painted” with copper sulfate to improve their appearance.Other items included 10,000 liters of “fake or adulterated” alcoholic drinks, adulterated honey and, in Indonesia, 70 kilograms of chicken intestines soaking in formalin, a prohibited additive.Officials in Belgium confiscated several kilograms of monkey meat from a traveler's luggage labeled for human consumption, and customs officers in France seized and destroyed 11 kilograms of locusts and 20 kilograms of caterpillars.

Former President George W. Bush held a meeting with a group of Democrats to discuss the current situation in Iraq. The meeting, which took place at Bush's ranch in Texas, was described as productive and focused on finding bipartisan solutions to the ongoing conflict in the region.Bush, who served as President from 2001 to 2009, has been vocal in his support for continued U.S. involvement in Iraq. He has expressed concern about the rise of extremist groups in the region and the potential threat they pose to stability and security.The meeting with Democrats, including several members of Congress, is seen as a significant step towards building consensus on how to address the challenges in Iraq. Both sides have expressed a willingness to work together to find common ground and develop a strategy for moving forward.While there were no specific details provided about the discussions that took place during the meeting, sources close to the former President indicate that a range of topics related to Iraq were covered. These included the role of U.S. military forces, diplomatic efforts, and potential aid and support for the Iraqi government.Overall, the meeting between Bush and Democrats signals a willingness to put aside partisan differences in order to address the complex and urgent issues facing Iraq. It remains to be seen what concrete actions will result from the discussions, but the engagement between the two sides is a positive development in efforts to find a path towards peace and stability in the region.

In the midst of ongoing clashes in the West Bank, Jerusalem witnessed peaceful prayers on Thursday. Despite the tension and violence in nearby areas, worshippers gathered at the Al-Aqsa Mosque for evening prayers without any incidents. This comes after days of violent confrontations between Israeli forces and Palestinian protesters in the West Bank, resulting in several injuries and arrests. The peaceful prayers in Jerusalem serve as a reminder of the importance of unity and faith in times of turmoil. Leaders on both sides have called for calm and dialogue to prevent further escalation of the conflict. The situation remains tense, but the peaceful prayers offer a glimmer of hope for a peaceful resolution to the ongoing unrest in the region.

The Trump administration is demanding answers from Cuba about eight detainees it says are political prisoners held by the communist government.In an open letter to Cuba's foreign minister released on Tuesday, Secretary of State Mike Pompeo said Cuba had refused to respond to multiple U.S. queries about the eight, who he said were illustrative of more than 100 political prisoners in Cuba. The list includes members of dissident groups and a journalist.Pompeo accused Cuba of reneging on promises to release them and other prisoners of conscience that date to the Obama administration and ignoring requests to even discuss them. He recalled that in October, Cuban delegates disrupted an event at the United Nations when U.S. representatives sought to raise the issue of political prisoners, an incident for which Pompeo has sought a U.N. investigation into alleged property damage."

The United States recently announced new produce safety rules aimed at preventing illness outbreaks related to fruits and vegetables. The goal of these rules is to enhance the safety of produce and reduce the risk of foodborne illnesses.The new regulations require producers to implement measures to prevent contamination of fruits and vegetables at various stages of production, from planting to harvesting to packaging. This includes ensuring proper hygiene practices, water quality, and sanitation in processing facilities.By enforcing these rules, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) hopes to decrease the number of illness outbreaks caused by contaminated produce. This is especially important as the consumption of fruits and vegetables continues to increase, making it crucial to ensure the safety of these foods for consumers.The FDA will be working closely with produce producers to help them understand and comply with the new regulations. The agency also plans to conduct inspections and monitor compliance to ensure that the rules are being followed effectively.Overall, the goal of the new produce safety rules is to protect public health by reducing the risk of illness outbreaks linked to fruits and vegetables. By implementing these regulations, the US aims to improve the safety of produce and provide consumers with confidence in the food they eat.

RANGOON — After months of delays, Burma's information ministry this week kept its pledge to lift part of its decades-old censorship policy. While reporters and editors grapple with the transition, many say a free press has not yet arrived. This week, newspapers in Burma will hit newsstands without ministry censorship for the first time since 1962. For the staff at The Myanmar Times, a Rangoon-based weekly, this is the first editorial meeting after the announced end of censorship. Editor Zaw Myint is elated, because not having to deal with censors will save time and improve the quality of reporting. "This is the same to my wedding day or something like that, but I will be more happy when we are granted as a daily license," he said. "Without censorship rules, with the chance to run our paper with a daily cycle, at that time I will be happier." State-run newspapers are still the only ones allowed to publish daily, but private publishers hope to be granted daily licenses. A released political prisoner and blogger who goes by the pen name Nay Phone Latt says online media in Burma remain unprotected. He says bloggers are still vulnerable to prosecution under electronic laws banning anti-government material on computers. "In the current media law, there is no place for the online media, so we are not safe," he said. "The electronic law still exists, so we are not safe according to this law." Controversial editor Kyaw Min Swe fears that the newly formed press council will not necessarily protect journalists and censorship will continue, in the form of legal threats. "They changed the system of PSB [Press Scrutiny Board] pre-censor to post-censor so that we have to submit after publication.

Mexican politician Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador has been declared the President-Elect of Mexico after winning the presidential election held on July 1st. The National Electoral Institute announced that Lopez Obrador, commonly known as AMLO, received over 53% of the votes, a historic margin in modern Mexican history.The defeated candidate, Ricardo Anaya, conceded the election and congratulated Lopez Obrador on his victory. President Enrique Pena Nieto also extended his congratulations to the President-Elect, wishing him success in his future endeavors.Lopez Obrador ran on a platform of fighting corruption, reducing violence, and improving the economy for all Mexicans. His victory represents a significant shift in Mexican politics, as he is the first leftist president in recent memory. The inauguration of Lopez Obrador is set to take place on December 1st, when he will officially become the leader of Mexico.

Iranian lawyers have joined the global backlash against the recent sentencing of human rights defender Nasrin Sotoudeh in Iran. Sotoudeh, a prominent lawyer who has been a vocal advocate for women's and children's rights, was recently sentenced to a total of 38 years in prison and 148 lashes.The sentencing of Sotoudeh has sparked outrage among the legal community in Iran, with many lawyers condemning the harsh punishment as a violation of human rights and an attack on the freedom of expression. In a statement released by the Iranian Bar Association, lawyers expressed their solidarity with Sotoudeh and called for her immediate release.The international community has also been quick to denounce the sentencing of Sotoudeh. Amnesty International has called the verdict a "shocking injustice" and urged Iranian authorities to overturn the decision. The European Union has also condemned the sentencing, describing it as a "flagrant violation of human rights".Sotoudeh has a long history of defending human rights in Iran, including representing women who have protested against the compulsory wearing of the hijab. She was previously imprisoned in 2010 for her work as a human rights lawyer.Despite facing immense pressure and persecution, Sotoudeh has remained steadfast in her commitment to defending the rights of others. The support of Iranian lawyers and the global community is a testament to the power of solidarity in the fight for justice and human rights.

A Ugandan researcher has been charged for allegedly insulting President Yoweri Museveni over the issue of sanitary pads. The researcher, whose name has not been disclosed, was arrested after making comments about the government's lack of action in providing free sanitary pads to girls in schools.The incident has sparked a debate about freedom of speech in Uganda, with many questioning the government's motives for cracking down on criticism. Some activists have argued that the charges are a blatant attempt to silence dissent and stifle public discourse.The government has defended its actions, stating that the researcher's comments were disrespectful and amounted to defamation of the president. However, critics argue that the charges are a clear violation of the right to freedom of expression.This case highlights the ongoing challenges faced by journalists and activists in Uganda, where the government has been accused of cracking down on dissent and restricting freedom of speech. The researcher is currently awaiting trial, and the outcome of the case is being closely monitored by human rights organizations.

According to officials, two-thirds of Iraqi expatriates have already voted in the country's parliamentary election on the first two days of balloting. The voting took place in 30 countries around the world, with a total of 1.2 million expatriates eligible to participate. The election is being closely watched as Iraq continues to face political instability and security challenges. The high voter turnout among expatriates is seen as a positive sign for the country's democratic process. The remaining expatriates still have the opportunity to cast their votes in the coming days before the election results are announced.

In a surprising turn of events, South Sudanese basketball player Akok Akok was not selected in the NBA Draft held yesterday. Akok, who was projected to be a late first-round pick, did not hear his name called by any of the teams participating in the draft.Many analysts believed that Akok's impressive performance in college would make him an attractive prospect for NBA teams. The 6'9" forward played for the University of Connecticut and showed great potential during his time there.Despite not being drafted, Akok remains hopeful for his future in basketball. He is expected to explore other options, including signing with a team as an undrafted free agent or pursuing a career overseas.South Sudanese basketball fans were disappointed by the news, as they were looking forward to seeing one of their own make it to the NBA. However, they remain supportive of Akok and are confident that he will continue to work hard and achieve his dream of playing at the highest level.While Akok may have been passed over in this year's draft, his determination and talent are sure to catch the eye of basketball scouts in the future. We wish him the best of luck in his basketball journey.

A teenager who was reportedly "infatuated" with the Columbine High School massacre has been found dead in Colorado, according to authorities.The 18-year-old woman, identified as Sol Pais, was said to have made threats that prompted the closure of schools in the Denver area earlier in the week. She was found dead from an apparent self-inflicted gunshot wound on Wednesday near Mount Evans in Clear Creek County.Pais had traveled from Miami to Colorado and purchased a shotgun and ammunition upon her arrival. The FBI had been conducting a massive manhunt for her before her body was discovered.The threats made by Pais had put the community on edge, with schools being closed as a precaution. The 20th anniversary of the Columbine High School massacre was on Saturday, prompting increased vigilance in the area.Authorities have not yet determined a motive for Pais' actions, but they are continuing to investigate the circumstances surrounding her death.

In the 1960s, researchers discovered a revolutionary formula that has saved the lives of millions of children around the world: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS). ORS is a deceptively simple combination of natural sugars, table salt and small amounts of necessary chemicals such as potassium. It's cheap, and it's even possible to make ORS at home. But, as Rose Hoban reports, it's not always the solution of choice for some parents.Mixed with water, ORS is given to children with gastroenteritis ? diarrhea or vomiting ? and is usually more effective at treating these problems than any other drug.But doctors have long noted that some parents don't give ORS to their children. Instead, they give them carbonated drinks that are no longer fizzy. Doctor Ashley Reece, a pediatric emergency physician at Watford General Hospital in Hertfordshire, England, sees this use of "flat" soda frequently. He says there's a public myth that flat carbonated drinks are good for kids with diarrhea and vomiting. "It's something that we would call an old wives' tale," Reece says, noting that family members or neighbors might pass along the advice.Reece thought that this use of carbonated drinks was inappropriate. So, he and a colleague set out to review all the literature they could find on how useful carbonated drinks, such as colas, really are for children with diarrhea or vomiting. They reviewed dozens of articles and found no evidence that carbonated drinks are effective for treating children with gastroenteritis."What we did find was a lots of evidence about the biochemical composition of carbonated drinks, [as] compared to oral rehydration solution," Reece says.

A group of displaced and adopted Native Americans are seeking to reconnect with their cultural roots and find their way back to their homeland. Known as the Lost Birds, these individuals were separated from their families and communities as children and placed into non-Native homes or boarding schools.The Lost Birds are now on a mission to reclaim their heritage and identity by tracing their ancestry and locating their relatives. Many of them have experienced feelings of isolation and disconnection from their Native culture and are determined to rediscover their roots.Through the use of genealogy research, social media, and community outreach, the Lost Birds are making strides in their journey to reunite with their families and communities. Some have already been able to locate their relatives and establish connections with their tribal nations.The process of reuniting with their families and reclaiming their heritage is not without challenges, but the Lost Birds remain resilient and determined. They are supported by various organizations and individuals who are dedicated to helping them navigate the complexities of their search for identity and belonging.As the Lost Birds continue their quest to find their way home, they are shedding light on the long-lasting effects of forced assimilation and displacement experienced by Native American communities. Their stories serve as a powerful reminder of the importance of preserving and honoring Indigenous cultures and traditions.

The Nigerian military reported fresh fighting with Boko Haram insurgents on Monday, a day after the group seized an army barracks in northeastern Borno state and attacked the state capital, Maiduguri.Defense spokesman General Chris Olukolade told VOA that an "operation involving air and land assets is underway" to retake the barracks in Monguno, about 180 kilometers northwest of Maiduguri.Towns of Monguno and Konduga, in Borna state, NigeriaAlso, the army said on Twitter that troops battled militants Sunday night and early Monday in Konduga, a town about 25 kilometers southeast of Maiduguri.The military said "ground troops are in charge" of the area and "substantial amounts of heavy weapons" have been captured. It said both sides suffered casualties.Reuters news agency reported that local journalist Bello Dukku counted more than 100 bodies of people killed in Sunday's fighting at a morgue in Maiduguri. Dukku said the dead included at least 15 soldiers and a few civilians.On Sunday, Nigerian officials said soldiers repelled three separate attempts by insurgents to enter Maiduguri.A security officer said each attempt was from a different direction.

A group of former diplomats have issued a warning to Secretary of State Mike Pompeo, advising him against cutting the US Refugee Bureau. These ex-diplomats believe that such a move would have negative repercussions on America's reputation and national security.The US Refugee Bureau plays a crucial role in providing assistance and protection to refugees who are seeking asylum in the United States. By cutting funding or resources to this bureau, the ex-diplomats argue that the US would be turning its back on some of the world's most vulnerable populations.In a joint statement, the former diplomats emphasized the importance of upholding America's commitment to humanitarian values and international law. They also highlighted the role that refugees have played in contributing to the cultural and economic fabric of the United States.The warning comes amid reports that the Trump administration is considering reducing the budget of the US Refugee Bureau as part of broader efforts to cut government spending. Critics fear that such a decision could have far-reaching consequences, both at home and abroad.As the debate continues, it remains to be seen whether Secretary Pompeo will heed the advice of these ex-diplomats or move forward with plans to cut the US Refugee Bureau.

The U.S. Navy sailed two ships through the Taiwan Strait on Wednesday, a move likely to upset China.A destroyer and an oil ship made the journey.This is the third time this year the Navy carried out what it calls a "routine transit.""The ships' transit through the Taiwan Strait demonstrates the U.S. commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific," the U.S. Pacific Fleet said in a statement. "The U.S. Navy will continue to fly, sail and operate anywhere international law allows."A U.S. official told the French news agency, Agence France-Presse, that Chinese ships were standing by and monitoring the American vessels but that both sides acted in a manner that was "safe and professional."China protested earlier sailings as a challenge to its sovereignty. It regards Taiwan as part of mainland China.

The 2016 U.S. presidential election is a year way, but voters throughout the country are voting Tuesday to fill state and local government positions and in referendums on issues affecting their communities.Voters in two states are electing governors. Pre-election polls show Republican Governor Phil Bryant headed to easy re-election in the southern state of Mississippi. In the mid-south state of Kentucky, however, Democratic state Attorney General Jack Conway and conservative Republican businessman Matt Bevin are locked in a tight contest.Voters in the large Midwest state of Ohio, long a bellwether in presidential elections, are deciding whether to legalize the recreational and medicinal use of marijuana. If approved, the state would become the first in the culturally conservative central part of the country to sanction marijuana use, and the fifth to do so across the United States. The Ohio ballot issue is unusual in that it would authorize growing marijuana on 10 farms owned by prominent investors, rather than allowing individuals to grow their own for personal use, as has occurred in other states where use of the drug has been legalized. Polls show Ohio voters are split evenly on the issue.In the western state of Colorado, where marijuana use is already legal, voters are deciding what do with $66 million in tax revenue generated by the sale of recreational marijuana.Non-discrimination protections In the southern city of Houston, Texas — the fourth largest city in the United States — voters are deciding whether to approve non-discrimination protections for gay and transgender people.

A top Russian general says his forces will remain in parts of Georgia, and he accuses Tbilisi of preparing a new wave of attacks in South Ossetia.Russian General Anatoly Nogovitsyn told reporters in Moscow Saturday that his country's forces will continue to patrol the Black Sea port city of Poti, as well as other areas that lie outside a designated security zone. Nogovitsyn also warned that Russia could increase the size of its force in Georgia if the United States begins to help Georgia rebuild its military.The Russian general defended Russia's actions, saying they were in agreement with the terms of a French-brokered cease-fire. He also accused Georgia's special services of preparing further actions in South Ossetia.Earlier, Georgian officials and witnesses said Russian forces are still entrenched north of the key city of Gori and near the town of Senaki.Hundreds of Georgians have been taking to the streets to express their unhappiness with Russia's continued presence, waving flags near Russian posts in Gori and Poti.The Georgian parliament voted unanimously Saturday to extend emergency wartime powers for President Mikheil Saakashvili for another 15 days.Meanwhile, the leader of South Ossetia, Eduard Kokoity, says he will visit Moscow Saturday.Kokoity says he will ask Russia to recognize South Ossetia's independence.The latest developments have again put Russia at odds with the West.U.S.

Civil servants in South Sudan are currently facing financial uncertainty as they wait for their overdue paychecks. According to reports, many government employees have not received their salaries for several months, leaving them struggling to make ends meet.The delay in payment has been attributed to the country's ongoing economic crisis, which has been exacerbated by political instability and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. Inflation has soared, making it increasingly difficult for civil servants to afford basic necessities such as food and shelter.The lack of payment has also taken a toll on the morale of government workers, many of whom are frustrated and disillusioned with the situation. Some have resorted to borrowing money from friends and family, while others have been forced to take on additional jobs to supplement their income.The South Sudanese government has acknowledged the issue and has promised to address the delay in payment. However, with the country facing a multitude of challenges, it remains to be seen how quickly the situation will be resolved.In the meantime, civil servants in South Sudan continue to wait anxiously for their paychecks, hoping that they will soon receive the financial support they desperately need.

TAIPEI, TAIWAN - Discovery of a nearly 70-year-old map of the South China Sea could help Beijing support its claim in Asia’s biggest maritime sovereignty dispute, but it would face criticism abroad and fall outside the scope of international law, experts say.The 1951 map, as described in an article by five Chinese researchers, includes coordinates in the 3.5 million-square-kilometer sea that offer Beijing a clearer-than-ever show of where its waters and islets lie, maritime scholars say. Today China uses a U-shaped nine-dash line that leaves spaces between those lines open to interpretation.Chinese officials have not so far used the map to change policy. They might be waiting to see what decision-makers elsewhere think, said Euan Graham, international security director with the Lowy Institute for International Policy.Brunei, Malaysia, the Philippines, Taiwan and Vietnam also claim all or parts of the sea that’s prized for fisheries, oil and gas. China has irritated them all over the past decade by landfilling disputed islets for military use.International courts would probably reject the map as a legal tool, Graham said.“Maps don’t carry independent legal weight. You can submit as many maps as you like, but the map changes nothing as regards the legality of claim,” he said. “It’s part of a broader campaign that China has long pursued, which is to build an evidential base amongst scholars that this is not a confected recent claim.”FILE - Philippine Foreign Secretary Albert Del Rosario points to an ancient map that officials said show that China's territorial claims over the South China Sea did not include the Scarborough Shoal.?A more vivid demarcation lineThe map, as described by the scholars in an undated 2018 Science China Press article, fills in gaps between nine thick lines that China uses now to describe the boundary around its disputed claims extending from Hong Kong to Borneo.

WASHINGTON - New Jersey Sen. Cory Booker voiced support Monday for former U.S. Vice President Joe Biden's bid for the Democratic presidential nomination, his latest former challenger to endorse him just ahead of six state primary elections he faces Tuesday against Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders.Booker, who dropped out of the party's presidential contest in January before voting began a couple weeks later, said in a Twitter comment, “The answer to hatred & division is to reignite our spirit of common purpose. @JoeBiden won’t only win - he’ll show there’s more that unites us than divides us. He’ll restore honor to the Oval Office and tackle our most pressing challenges. That’s why I’m proud to endorse Joe.”Booker's endorsement came a day after California Sen. Kamala Harris, who dropped out of the contest in December, endorsed Biden, saying that she felt that he was best prepared to "steer America through these turbulent times."Harris, often mentioned by U.S. political analysts as one of several possible vice-presidential running mates with Biden, said the U.S. needs a president "who reflects the decency and dignity of the American people, a president who speaks the truth; and a president who fights for those whose voices are too often overlooked or ignored."Former Foe, Sen. Kamala Harris, Endorses Biden Presidential BidVanquished Democratic opponents are supporting the former vice president's campaign against TrumpSeveral former rivals have now endorsed Biden, a list that also includes former South Bend, Indiana Mayor Pete Buttigieg and Minnesota Senator Amy Klobuchar.

A team of researchers from the University of Cambridge has developed a new technique to detect water on planets in other solar systems. This breakthrough could potentially lead to the discovery of habitable exoplanets outside of our own solar system.The new technique involves analyzing the light that passes through the atmospheres of exoplanets. By studying how different molecules in the atmosphere absorb and scatter light, the researchers are able to determine the presence of water vapor.Dr. Sarah Johnson, lead researcher on the project, explained that this new technique could revolutionize the way we search for water on exoplanets. "Water is essential for life as we know it, so finding water on other planets is a key step in the search for habitable worlds," she said.The team tested their technique on a known exoplanet with a water-rich atmosphere and were able to successfully detect the presence of water vapor. This promising result has excited the scientific community and raised hopes for finding potentially habitable exoplanets in the near future.The researchers plan to continue refining their technique and applying it to a wider range of exoplanets in the coming months. With advancements like these, the search for extraterrestrial life beyond our own solar system is becoming more promising than ever before.

TOKYO - While again stating there are no plans to cancel or delay the Tokyo Olympics, local organizers postponed training sessions for volunteers on Friday for at least two months because of the virus outbreak spreading from China.About 80,000 people are needed at the Olympics to provide free work which the IOC calls “key support to ensure the success of the games.”Tokyo organizers said the postponed sessions are “part of efforts to prevent the spread of infection of the novel coronavirus.”Training will be postponed until May or later, organizers said on their website. More than 200,000 people applied to be volunteers, with about one-third from outside Japan.The Olympics are scheduled to run from July 24-Aug. 9.The International Olympic Committee, local organizers and the World Health Organization have repeatedly said there is no current need to put the games in doubt.The virus, known as COVID-19, has caused the deaths of about 2,250 people since it emerged in the Chinese city Wuhan late last year. Up to Friday, three deaths and more than 700 cases — most from a quarantined cruise ship docked in Tokyo Bay — had been reported in Japan.“

President Donald Trump announced today that a new trade deal has been reached with Canada and Mexico, which he described as "terrific" for all three countries involved. The deal, called the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), is set to replace the current North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) that has been in place since 1994.Trump praised the agreement, stating that it will benefit American farmers, workers, and businesses by creating a more level playing field for trade. The USMCA includes provisions on agriculture, intellectual property, digital trade, and labor rights, among other things.The announcement of the trade deal comes after months of negotiations between the three countries, with Trump previously threatening to pull out of NAFTA if a new agreement could not be reached. The USMCA still needs to be ratified by the legislatures of all three countries before it can officially go into effect.Overall, the new trade deal marks a significant achievement for the Trump administration, which has made renegotiating trade agreements a key priority. Trump's positive response to the agreement with Canada and Mexico is likely to be met with approval from his supporters, as well as from those who have been critical of NAFTA in the past.

CHARLOTTE, NORTH CAROLINA - Democrats assembled in Charlotte, North Carolina to nominate President Barack Obama for a second term promised the event will be the “most open and accessible convention in history.” That is being made possible in part by the contributions of new media producers who are providing content to new audiences online. An experiment, known as the "PPL", is under the direction of Bruce Clark. He describes it as an alternative newsroom of sorts for the kind of media that either can’t afford, or can’t get into, traditional media spaces at the Democratic National Convention. “To juxtapose that against all the digital content creators, all the future of media, has been quite an interesting experiment," Clark explains. “The digital age has allowed us to tear down a lot of walls and allow access to folks who generally wouldn’t have access to information.” Those folks, working in a room just a few blocks from the convention, include bloggers, Instagrammers, Tweeters and independent filmmakers like Tim Grant. "New media has changed convention coverage just by the scope of the coverage," Grant says. "The ability to tweet what you see, you know, take a picture at any moment. It’s so broad now. You can type in hash tag DNC and you’re getting a hundred new tweets every other minute.” During the first night of the DNC, that figure was not in the hundreds, but in the thousands, setting new records, according to Twitter’s Adam Sharp. “Before Michelle Obama had even started her speech, she had broken the record for the Republican convention," Sharp notes. "Finishes her speech at 28,000 tweets per minute. Almost exactly double Mitt Romney, and interesting enough, double her husband’s performance at the State of the Union earlier this year.”

A new report says there is enough food in northern Mali to meet local demand, despite recent conflict in the region.The report from the U.S.-fundedsays traders from southern Mali, Algeria, and Niger continue to supply markets in northern Mali's main towns and cities.It says the situation is worse in more isolated rural areas, where it says emergency needs remain high.The report notes household incomes are down and food prices are up in northern Mali but predicts the overall food situation will improve because of a promising harvest and expanding humanitarian aid programs.Northern Mali was plunged into conflict early this year when Tuareg separatists renewed their rebellion.

The African Union (AU) is facing challenges in trying to finalize a peace deal in the conflict-ridden region of Darfur. Negotiations have been ongoing for months, but a final agreement has yet to be reached.The conflict in Darfur has been ongoing for years, with violence and instability plaguing the region. The AU has been working tirelessly to bring all parties to the table and facilitate discussions aimed at achieving a lasting peace agreement.However, issues such as power-sharing, wealth distribution, and security arrangements continue to be major sticking points in the negotiations. The various parties involved in the conflict have differing priorities and demands, making it difficult to find common ground.Despite the challenges, the AU remains committed to its efforts to secure a peace deal in Darfur. The organization is working closely with international partners and stakeholders to push for progress in the negotiations and ensure that all parties are committed to a peaceful resolution of the conflict.The situation in Darfur remains complex and volatile, but the AU is hopeful that a peace deal can be reached in the near future. The organization is urging all parties to prioritize the interests of the people of Darfur and work towards a peaceful and inclusive solution to the conflict.

Opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai has returned to Harare, Zimbabwe after receiving medical treatment in neighboring South Africa. Tsvangirai, who is the leader of the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC) party, has been battling cancer for the past two years.Upon his return, Tsvangirai expressed his determination to continue fighting for democracy and economic reform in Zimbabwe.

China has commuted the prison sentence of a dissident who was convicted of illegally funding subversive political activities, according to state media.The official Xinhua news agency says Wang Ce was released early from jail for good behavior.The 52-year-old was arrested in November 1998 on charges arising from a $1,000 payment Mr. Wang allegedly made to another activist who was later charged with illegally setting up the China Democracy Party. Under the Chinese political system, the Communist Party does not allow any opposition groups to exist. Mr. Wang was serving a four-year prison sentence.Mr. Wang's sister told the French news agency that Mr. Wang left China to return to Spain, where he had lived in the 1990s.Mr. Wang was one of numerous political activists jailed in late 1998 and early 1999 in a crackdown on dissent. The Chinese president, Jiang Zemin, had vowed to stamp out threats to the ruling Communist Party.

North Korea has condemned the dispatch of a US aircraft carrier to the region as "outrageous" and a provocation. The USS Carl Vinson is being deployed to waters off the Korean Peninsula as a show of force against North Korea's nuclear and missile programs.North Korea's state-run media accused the US of escalating tensions in the region and warned of a "merciless response" to any provocation. The country's military said they are prepared to retaliate with their own show of force if necessary.The US has defended the move as a necessary measure to ensure the security of the region and its allies. The deployment of the aircraft carrier is seen as a response to North Korea's continued missile tests and threats of nuclear attacks.Tensions in the region have been high in recent months, with both sides engaging in heated rhetoric and military displays. The US has also been conducting joint military exercises with South Korea, further angering North Korea.It remains to be seen how North Korea will respond to the presence of the USS Carl Vinson in the region, but the situation is certainly tense as both sides continue to flex their military muscles.

In a groundbreaking achievement, SpaceX successfully landed one of its rocket boosters on a drone ship at sea. The Falcon 9 rocket booster, which had been used in a previous mission, made a precise landing on the ship, marking the first time SpaceX has been able to recover a booster in this manner.The successful landing is a significant milestone for SpaceX, as the company continues to make advancements in reusable rocket technology. Being able to recover and reuse rocket boosters is a key part of SpaceX's mission to reduce the cost of space travel and make it more accessible.The booster is expected to be refurbished and used in a future mission, further demonstrating the potential cost-saving benefits of reusable rocket technology. SpaceX founder and CEO Elon Musk has previously stated that reusing rocket components is essential for the company to achieve its long-term goal of making Mars colonization a reality.The successful landing comes after a series of failed attempts by SpaceX to recover rocket boosters at sea. The company has been working tirelessly to perfect its landing technology, and this latest achievement is a testament to their dedication and innovation.Overall, the breakthrough landing represents a major step forward in the development of reusable rocket technology, and bodes well for the future of space exploration. SpaceX continues to push the boundaries of what is possible in space travel, and this latest accomplishment is sure to inspire and excite space enthusiasts around the world.

In preparation for the upcoming Tour de France, a total of 23,000 police officers have been mobilized to ensure the safety and security of the event. The annual cycling race, which is set to begin on June 26th, attracts thousands of spectators and participants from around the world.The increased police presence is in response to recent security concerns, as large-scale sporting events are often targets for potential threats. Authorities are taking extra precautions to prevent any incidents that could disrupt the race or put participants at risk.In addition to the massive police force, organizers of the Tour de France have also implemented strict COVID-19 protocols to protect the health and well-being of all involved.

Fans around the world are coming together to remember the King of Rock and Roll, Elvis Presley, on the 30th anniversary of his death. The legendary singer passed away on August 16, 1977, but his legacy continues to live on through his music and impact on popular culture.From his early hits like "Hound Dog" and "Jailhouse Rock" to his iconic performances in films such as "Blue Hawaii" and "Viva Las Vegas," Elvis captivated audiences with his charisma and unique sound. His influence can still be seen today in artists across various genres.Events are being held in Memphis, Tennessee, where Elvis lived at his famous Graceland mansion, as well as around the world to honor his memory. Fans are sharing stories of how Elvis touched their lives and celebrating the music that made him a global superstar.Thirty years after his untimely passing, Elvis Presley remains a cultural icon and a beloved figure in music history. His music continues to inspire new generations of fans and his memory will forever live on in the hearts of those who appreciate his enduring legacy.

The deadly California fire season continues to rage on with no sign of letting up. Multiple wildfires have been burning across the state, destroying homes, businesses, and forcing thousands of residents to evacuate.The largest of the fires, the Dixie Fire, has been burning for weeks and has already consumed over 500,000 acres. Firefighters are working tirelessly to contain the blaze, but strong winds and dry conditions have made their efforts challenging.In addition to the Dixie Fire, several other smaller fires have broken out in various parts of the state, including the Caldor Fire near Lake Tahoe and the River Fire in Placer County.Governor Gavin Newsom has declared a state of emergency and requested additional resources from the federal government to help combat the fires. The National Guard has been deployed to assist with evacuations and firefighting efforts.The ongoing fire season has taken a heavy toll on California, with many communities facing the devastation of losing their homes and livelihoods. As the fires continue to burn, residents are urged to stay vigilant, heed evacuation orders, and take precautions to prevent further fires from breaking out.

Churches and opposition leaders in South Africa are criticizing President Jacob Zuma's leadership after he admitted to fathering a child out of wedlock.
