It is the biggest-ever and the best trade deal in the world – that’s how U.S. President Donald Trump and members of his administration are touting a new trade pact they have reached with neighbors Canada and Mexico.“This landmark agreement will send cash and jobs pouring into the United States and into North America,” predicted Trump.After intense last-minute discussions just ahead of a self-imposed Sunday midnight deadline, the United States and Canada announced they had reached a deal, allowing a modified three-way pact with Mexico to replace the nearly quarter-century-old North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA)."This is a terrific deal for all of us," Trump said in a victory speech in the White House Rose Garden where he was surrounded by several members of his Cabinet and his trade negotiators who had worked to achieve the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA).The pact, underpinning $1.2 trillion in annual trade, is based on “fairness and reciprocity,” according to Trump, who added that it was reached after disagreements with his counterparts in Mexico City and Ottawa, Enrique Pena Nieto and Justin Trudeau.Trump said he spoke to both leaders on Monday.The agreement will consolidate the North American region “as one of the most competitive in the world,” according to the Mexican president on Twitter.“

A recent poll conducted across Africa has revealed that Swazi King Mswati III is among the continent's least popular leaders. The poll, conducted by a reputable research firm, surveyed citizens from various African countries to gauge their opinions on their respective leaders.The results showed that King Mswati III of Eswatini ranked near the bottom in terms of popularity, with a significant number of respondents expressing dissatisfaction with his leadership. Many cited concerns over his authoritarian rule, human rights abuses, and extravagant lifestyle as reasons for their disapproval.The poll also highlighted a growing trend of dissatisfaction with traditional monarchies in Africa, as citizens increasingly demand more democratic governance and accountability from their leaders. While some supporters of the monarchy defended King Mswati III, arguing that he has brought stability and economic development to Eswatini, the poll results indicate a significant level of discontent among the population.It remains to be seen how these poll results will impact King Mswati III's rule and whether they will lead to any changes in his leadership style. As dissatisfaction with traditional leaders continues to grow across the continent, it is clear that African citizens are increasingly demanding more responsive and accountable governance from their leaders.

Sudan's parliament has decided to reduce the state of emergency in the country to a period of 6 months, following months of political turmoil and civil unrest. The decision was made in an effort to restore stability and peace in the country after the ousting of former President Omar al-Bashir.The state of emergency was initially declared in April of this year, following widespread protests calling for al-Bashir's resignation. The move to shorten the state of emergency period comes after pressure from various political parties and civil society groups, who argued that the extended period of emergency rule was impeding the country's transition to democracy.While the decision to reduce the state of emergency is seen as a positive step towards normalizing the situation in Sudan, many are still calling for further reforms and the establishment of a civilian-led government. The country is currently being governed by a transitional military council, which took power after al-Bashir's ousting.The parliament's decision to limit the state of emergency to 6 months is seen as a compromise between the military and civilian factions in Sudan, with hopes that it will pave the way for a peaceful transition to a democratic government. Time will tell whether this decision will lead to lasting stability and progress in the country.

The Pakistani Army has denied allegations of meddling in the upcoming elections and has announced plans to deploy troops to guard the voting process. This decision comes amidst increasing concerns about election security and potential interference.The Chief of Army Staff, General Qamar Javed Bajwa, reiterated the military's commitment to ensuring a free and fair election. He stated that the army's role would be limited to maintaining peace and security during the voting period, and that troops would only be deployed at the request of the Election Commission of Pakistan.The announcement comes after opposition parties raised concerns about the army's involvement in the electoral process, accusing them of backing certain political parties. The military's decision to deploy troops is seen as a measure to address these concerns and ensure transparency in the upcoming elections.The Election Commission of Pakistan welcomed the army's decision to deploy troops, stating that it would help maintain law and order and protect the integrity of the electoral process. With tensions running high ahead of the elections, the presence of troops is expected to provide a sense of security and deter any potential attempts to disrupt the voting process.As Pakistan prepares for the upcoming elections, the deployment of troops by the army is seen as a positive step towards ensuring a peaceful and fair electoral process. Voters are hopeful that their voices will be heard and that the election results will reflect the will of the people.

Germans across the country are praising a group of refugees who helped catch a suspected bomber in Cologne. The refugees, who have been dubbed "hero refugees" by many, acted quickly and bravely when they noticed a man behaving suspiciously near a train station.The suspect, a 16-year-old Syrian boy, was seen carrying a homemade bomb in a backpack. The refugees immediately alerted authorities and worked together to detain the suspect until the police arrived. Thanks to their quick thinking and decisive actions, a potential disaster was averted.This incident comes at a time when tensions surrounding immigration and refugees are high in Germany. Many see this as a powerful example of how refugees can contribute positively to their host communities and play a crucial role in maintaining safety and security.The actions of these "hero refugees" have not gone unnoticed, with many Germans expressing their gratitude and admiration for their bravery. It serves as a reminder that in times of crisis, unity and cooperation can make all the difference.

Recent technological advances demonstrated by China have started an intense debate on whether it is set to take a lead in the field of artificial intelligence, or AI, which has extensive business and military applications.U.S. concerns about China's AI advances have also influenced, in part, the ongoing trade negotiations between Washington and Beijing. Both the United States and European Union are taking measures to stop information leaks that are reportedly helping Chinese companies at the expense of Western business.But many analysts are saying that Chinese corporate and defense-related research in areas like AI and 5G wireless technologies can thrive on their own even if information from the Western world is shut off. China is already reportedly leading in several segments of businesses like autonomous vehicles, facial recognition and certain kinds of drones.FILE - People retrieve their parcels from a JD.com driverless delivery robot in Tianjin, China, Nov. 12, 2018.The U.S.-based Allen Institute of Artificial Intelligence recently captured attention when it reported that China is a close second after the United States when it comes to producing frequently-cited research papers on artificial intelligence. The U.S. contribution is 29%, and China accounts for 26% of such papers."The U.S. still is ahead in AI development capabilities, but the gap between the U.S. and China is closing rapidly because of the significant new AI investments in China," Bart Selman, president-elect of the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence, a professional organization, told VOA.Chinese President Xi Jinping has in recent months encouraged Communist leaders to "ensure that our country marches in the front ranks when it comes to theoretical research in this important area of AI, and occupies the high ground in critical and AI core technologies." He also asked them to "ensure that critical and core AI technologies are firmly grasped in our own hands."

The battle over reforming the U.S. health care system has intensified in recent weeks, and the political stakes are growing for President Barack Obama as well as for Democrats and Republicans in Congress.Grass roots democracy can be chaotic at times, and members of Congress are getting a first-hand glimpse of the anger and fear that is dominating many of their town hall forums across the country to discuss health care."This is not health reform, this is control! Control over our lives!" shouted one man at a recent meeting in Iowa. But raucous crowds are showing up at town halls all across the country.Pennsylvania Senator Arlen Specter, a Democrat, had his hands full trying to calm down a man in Lebanon, Pennsylvania, who was angry about being denied a chance to ask a question.SPECTER: "Now wait a minute. Now wait a minute! Wait a minute. You want to leave?"MAN: "Well, I got news for you. You and your cronies in the government do this kind of stuff all the time. One day God is going to stand before you and he is going to judge you and the rest of your damn cronies up on the [Capitol] Hill, and then you will get your just desserts!"Passions are running high because reforming the U.S. health care system is an enormous undertaking that affects virtually all Americans, and because many of the proposals are politically polarizing.President Obama and his Democratic allies in Congress want to expand health-insurance coverage to 46 million Americans who currently do not have it. In addition, the administration wants to bring down the soaring cost of health care for consumers, businesses and the government.In an attempt to regain political momentum in the health-care debate, Mr.

An Israeli court has blocked a planned strike that was set to take place on November 3, 2003. The strike, organized by a labor union, was intended to protest against government policies that workers deemed unfair. However, the court ruled that the strike would pose a threat to the country's national security and ordered it to be canceled.The decision to block the strike has sparked mixed reactions, with some workers expressing disappointment and frustration, while others believe that the court made the right call. The government has stated that it is willing to engage in dialogue with the labor union to address their grievances in a peaceful and constructive manner.This is not the first time that labor disputes have arisen in Israel, as workers often struggle with issues such as low wages, lack of job security, and long working hours. The court's intervention in this case highlights the delicate balance between workers' rights and the need to maintain stability and security in the country.

Defense Secretary Chuck Hagel recently spoke about the ongoing challenges that US troops are facing in Afghanistan. Despite progress being made, Hagel emphasized that there are still obstacles that need to be overcome.One of the main challenges highlighted by Hagel is the continued threat posed by the Taliban and other insurgent groups in the region. These groups have been able to adapt to the changing security situation and continue to pose a threat to both Afghan forces and coalition troops.Additionally, the Afghan government still faces issues of corruption and instability, which have hindered efforts to establish lasting peace and security in the country. Hagel stressed the importance of addressing these issues in order to ensure the long-term success of the mission in Afghanistan.While the US has been able to significantly reduce its troop presence in Afghanistan in recent years, Hagel emphasized that a complete withdrawal of troops is not yet possible. He stated that a residual force will be necessary to continue training and supporting Afghan security forces, as well as conducting counterterrorism operations.Overall, Hagel's comments highlight the complex and challenging nature of the situation in Afghanistan, and the continued efforts that will be required to achieve lasting peace and stability in the region.

After a month-long hiatus, the Colombian government and the National Liberation Army (ELN) rebels have resumed peace talks in a bid to end more than five decades of conflict.The negotiations were put on hold in January after a series of deadly attacks by the ELN, including a car bombing in the capital city of Bogota that left more than 20 people dead.However, both sides have expressed a commitment to finding a peaceful resolution and have returned to the negotiating table in Ecuador. The talks are focused on issues such as social and economic development, political participation, and the demobilization of ELN fighters.The conflict between the Colombian government and various rebel groups has resulted in thousands of deaths and displaced millions of people since it began in the 1960s. The resumption of peace talks brings hope for a lasting peace in the region.Stay tuned for updates on the progress of the negotiations between Colombia and the ELN rebels.

In an effort to attract overseas professionals, two Chinese cities have announced new incentives for foreign talent looking to work and live in China. Hangzhou, a major city in eastern China known for its tech industry, has introduced a new policy offering financial incentives and support for foreign professionals in key industries such as artificial intelligence, digital economy, and biomedicine. The policy includes subsidies for housing, healthcare, and children's education, as well as streamlined visa processes and access to local social services.Meanwhile, Chengdu, a city in southwestern China, has also rolled out a new set of incentives aimed at attracting foreign talent. The city's policies include financial support for startups and innovation projects, as well as subsidies for housing and living expenses. Chengdu has been focusing on developing its high-tech industry and is hoping to leverage the expertise of overseas professionals to further drive innovation and economic growth in the region.These initiatives come as part of China's broader efforts to attract top talent from around the world and boost its reputation as a global hub for innovation and entrepreneurship. With cities like Hangzhou and Chengdu offering attractive incentives and support for foreign professionals, it is likely that more overseas talent will be drawn to China in the coming years.

According to a recent survey conducted by the American Chamber of Commerce in China, US tariffs are having a negative impact on American businesses operating in China. The survey found that a significant number of American companies in China are feeling the effects of the trade war between the US and China, with many reporting lower profits and increased costs due to the tariffs.Many of the American businesses surveyed cited the uncertainty surrounding the trade war as a major concern, with some expressing fears that the situation could escalate further and lead to even more severe consequences. Some companies have already been forced to make difficult decisions, such as cutting jobs or relocating their operations to other countries in order to mitigate the effects of the tariffs.Despite the challenges posed by the tariffs, some American businesses in China remain optimistic about the future and are actively seeking ways to adapt to the changing trade environment. However, the survey results highlight the real and immediate impact that the tariffs are having on American companies operating in China, underscoring the need for a resolution to the ongoing trade dispute between the US and China.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights says the Islamic State extremist group executed 33 people this week -- the first mass executions carried out by the militants this year.The British-based watchdog group says the killings were carried out in the eastern countryside of Deir Ezzor early Wednesday morning.The Observatory says its activists witnessed the executions, but says it is unknown if those killed were fighters on either side of the six-year civil war, or civilians who had been arrested by IS forces and sentenced to die.The Observatory says it is the largest mass execution carried out by IS since two groups of 15 persons were killed in two separate executions in between September and October of last year.

U.S. President George Bush is urging an end to the fighting in Georgia's breakaway province of South Ossetia. VOA White House correspondent Paula Wolfson reports from Beijing, where Mr. Bush is attending the Olympic Games.President Bush says the fighting must stop."I am deeply concerned about the situation in Georgia," he said. "The United States takes this matter very seriously."He says he is particularly troubled by the fact the conflict is spreading to other parts of Georgia - a reference to Russian bombings beyond the borders of South Ossetia."The violence is endangering regional peace," said President Bush. "Civilian lives have been lost and others are endangered."South Ossetia's autonomous status was abolished by the Georgian government in 1990, then reinstated in 1992. The current government in Tblisi has vowed to bring it back into the fold and on Thursday launched a military operation to do just that.But South Ossetia has strong ties to Moscow, which supports the separatist movement. When Georgia sent its forces in, the Russians responded by sending in more troops and bombers.President Bush says all sides need to step back, stop hostilities, and work with mediators to resolve the conflict."Georgia is a sovereign nation and its territorial integrity must be respected," he said. "We have urged an immediate halt to the violence and a stand-down by all troops."White House officials say President Bush re-enforced the U.S. position in separate conversations with Russian President Dmitri Medvedev, and Georgian President Mikhail Saakashvili.

In a recent speech, former President Barack Obama challenged states across the country to overhaul their education systems in order to better prepare students for the challenges of the future. Obama emphasized the importance of investing in education as a means of ensuring economic growth and social mobility. He called for states to prioritize funding for schools in low-income communities and to focus on closing the achievement gap between students of different backgrounds. The former President also stressed the need for innovative approaches to teaching and learning, pointing to the success of programs that focus on hands-on learning, personalized instruction, and the integration of technology in the classroom. Obama's call to action comes at a time when many states are struggling to provide high-quality education to all students, particularly those in underserved communities. By urging states to prioritize education reform, Obama hopes to inspire change and improve the outcomes for students across the country.

Zimbabwe has suspended exports of ostrich and other poultry after discovering a strain of avian flu that can be lethal to birds, but poses little risk to humans.Thursday, Zimbabwe's state-run newspaper The Herald reported that the H5N2 strain of avian flu was detected on two farms in Zimbabwe's Matabeleland province.It quotes the state veterinary services director, Dr. Stuart Hargreaves, as saying the virus was confirmed with samples sent to South Africa.The H5N2 strain of avian flu is not known to pass from birds to humans, unlike the H5N1 strain that has killed nearly 70 people in Southeast Asia since 2003.Dr.

A tragic plane crash in Colombia has left no survivors, authorities confirmed today. The aircraft, which was carrying passengers from various nationalities, went down in a remote area outside of the capital city. Emergency crews rushed to the scene but were unable to rescue any survivors. The cause of the crash is still unknown and an investigation is currently underway.

Israel is facing tough months ahead as pressure continues to mount on Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. Netanyahu is currently embroiled in multiple corruption scandals and facing mounting criticism for his handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. The Prime Minister's approval ratings have plummeted, and there have been calls for his resignation from both within his own party and the opposition.The pressure on Netanyahu is expected to increase as Israel gears up for national elections in March. Many political analysts believe that Netanyahu's hold on power is weakening, and that he may struggle to form a stable coalition government after the elections.In addition to the political challenges facing Netanyahu, Israel is also grappling with a surge in COVID-19 cases and a slow vaccine rollout. The country has been under strict lockdown measures for several weeks, leading to widespread frustration and anger among the population.As Israel navigates these challenges, the coming months are sure to be a testing time for the country and its leadership.

United Nations Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon is urging North Korea to heed the call of the international community by canceling an upcoming rocket launch.Ban spoke Thursday in Geneva, as North Korean engineers prepared to launch a rocket that Pyongyang says will carry a weather satellite into space. Western nations and regional neighbors accuse the North of using the launch as a ploy to test a ballistic missile that could later be fitted with a nuclear warhead.The U.N. chief repeated warnings that the launch, which is set to take place sometime between Thursday and Monday, will raise tensions in the region. He also said it "clearly" violates a U.N. Security Council resolution aimed at reining in North Korea's nuclear weapons program.Meanwhile, military forces in South Korea and Japan were on high alert Thursday on what the North says is the first day of a five-day window for the launch.Any plans for a Thursday launch dimmed by mid-day with no sign of new activity at the launch pad outside a cloud-covered Pyongyang. In Tokyo, Prime Minister Yoshihiko Noda repeated his nation's appeal for Pyongyang to cancel the launch but said Japanese forces are ready to shoot the rocket down if it strays over Japanese territory."In case it happens, we are on full alert. Up to the last minute, we urge North Korea to refrain from launching," said Noda.South Korea has also put its forces on heightened alert and threatened to shoot down the rocket if it appears likely to crash into South Korean territory.

A looming strike by construction workers in South Africa is posing a significant threat to the progress of World Cup projects in the country. The strike, led by the National Union of Mineworkers, is expected to involve over 60,000 workers and could potentially disrupt key construction projects, including stadiums, transportation infrastructure, and accommodation facilities.The strike comes as a major setback for South Africa, as the country prepares to host the FIFA World Cup in 2022. The event is highly anticipated and is expected to bring in a significant economic boost to the country. However, delays and disruptions caused by the strike could jeopardize the timely completion of crucial projects, potentially affecting the overall success of the tournament.Government officials and project stakeholders are currently in talks with union representatives in an effort to reach a resolution and prevent the strike from escalating further. The outcome of these discussions remains uncertain, but both sides are hopeful for a swift and amicable resolution to avoid any further delays in the construction of World Cup projects.As the situation continues to unfold, all eyes are on South Africa as the country works towards overcoming this challenge and ensuring that the World Cup projects are completed on schedule. Stay tuned for further updates on this developing story.

U.S. President Barack Obama is among dozens of heads of state who are in Colombia for the Summit of the Americas. The 33 presidents and prime ministers from Canada, the U.S., Caribbean, and Latin America are meeting in the resort town of Cartagena.President Obama is expected to use the summit as an opportunity to emphasize the importance of expanding economic and other ties across the region. While the U.S. president remains personally popular throughout the region, he faces growing discontent from his counterparts over a number of issues, including Washington's decades-long fight against the illicit drug trade and Cuba's continued absence from the summit.A growing number of leaders, including the summit's host, Colombian President Juan Manuel Santos, have called for decriminalizing drugs in order to reduce the demand fueling the violent drug cartels. Dan Restrepo, Mr. Obama's national security advisor for Latin America, says the president does not support decriminalization.There is also an increasing demand for the U.S. to end its five-decade-long embargo on Cuba and allow it to rejoin the Organization of American States. Ecuadorian President Rafael Correa is boycotting the summit to protest Cuba's absence and others are warning it should be the last without the communist-run island's participation. President Obama can also expect to be in the minority in his opposition to Argentina's claim to the British-controlled Falkland islands.

Ukrainian Prime Minister Arseniy Yatsenyuk is warning Russia that military intervention would mean war between the two neighbors, after the Russian parliament authorized the use of Russian troops in the southern Ukrainian region of Crimea.Acting Ukrainian President Oleksandr Turchynov said Saturday he has put the country's armed forces on combat alert following Saturday's vote in Russia. The approval of Russian President Vladimir Putin's request for military authorization made official what Ukraine's newly-established interim government has described as an ongoing deployment of Russian troops in Crimea, a Black Sea peninsula.The majority-Russian region, which became part of independent Ukraine when the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, has become the focus of turmoil that began months ago when Ukraine's now-ousted president Viktor Yanukovych rejected a deal with the European Union in favor of closer ties with Russia. The United Nations Security Council scheduled an emergency meeting on Ukraine Saturday, while European Union foreign ministers will hold emergency talks in Brussels on Monday.The situation has escalated into the biggest confrontation between Russia and the West since the Cold War.On Saturday, Ukraine's U.N. mission asked members of the Security Council to "do everything possible" to stop what it described as Russian "aggression" against Ukraine, calling Russia's actions a "severe violation of international law."U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon's spokesman said Saturday the U.N. chief is "gravely concerned about the deterioration of the situation" in Crimea and "reiterates his call for the full respect" for Ukraine's independence, sovereignty and territorial integrity. The spokesman said Mr. Ban would be speaking with President Putin soon about the situation.The United States has also urged Russia to respect Ukraine's territorial integrity. Russia's upper house of parliament recommended Saturday that Mr. Putin recall the Russian ambassador from Washington, a day after U.S. President Barack Obama warned of "costs" if Russia intervenes militarily in Ukraine.

In a surprising turn of events, President Macri's rivals in the upcoming Argentine election have struck an alliance with a centrist party. This move comes as a blow to Macri's campaign, as it unites opposition forces against him. The centrist party, known for its moderate policies and appeal to a wide range of voters, could potentially sway the election in favor of Macri's challengers.The alliance was announced in a joint press conference, where leaders from the opposition parties emphasized the need for change in Argentina's political landscape. They highlighted the country's economic challenges and promised to work together to address them effectively.President Macri, who is seeking re-election, has faced criticism for his handling of the economy and rising inflation rates. The alliance with the centrist party poses a significant threat to his chances of staying in power for another term.As the election draws closer, it remains to be seen how this alliance will impact the political landscape in Argentina. With the opposition forces united against President Macri, the race for the presidency is sure to be a closely contested one.

PANAMA CITY - A Panamanian court has granted bail to the two founders of Mossack Fonseca, the law firm at the center of the Panama Papers scandal, in a case allegedly tied to a sprawling corruption scandal in Brazil.Following their February arrest on charges of money laundering, Jurgen Mossack and Ramon Fonseca each paid $500,000 Friday and will be set free soon, the attorney for the two lawyers, Guillermina McDonald, told Reuters.McDonald said the court ruled the two were not a flight risk because they had been cooperating with the investigation.Another lawyer from the firm, Edison Teano, was still being held, she said.FILE - Partner of the Panama-based law firm Mossack Fonseca, Ramon Fonseca speaks during an interview at his office in Panama City,, April 7, 2016.Mossack Fonseca emerged from obscurity in April 2016 with the Panama Papers, millions of documents stolen from the firm and leaked to the media that illustrated how the wealthy use offshore corporations to avoid taxes.

NEW DELHI - In the most significant conviction relating to deadly anti-Sikh riots that swept India in 1984, a senior politician of the opposition Congress Party has been sentenced to life imprisonment.The Delhi High Court found Sajjan Kumar guilty of instigating mobs during the violence that followed the killing of then Prime Minister Indira Gandhi by her Sikh bodyguards.Kumar, now 73, was a member of parliament when nearly 3,000 Sikhs died in the carnage that erupted for four days as rampaging mobs brutally attacked and killed members of the minority Sikh community and torched their businesses.Calling the riots a “crime against humanity”, the judges said that “a majority of the perpetrators of these horrific mass crimes enjoyed political patronage and escaped trial.” They said that there is “need to assure the countless victims that truth will prevail."The verdict was widely welcomed by family members of the victims and Sikh activists, who have long accused leaders of the Congress Party of fanning the violence.Kumar, who was accused of leading a mob in a Delhi neighborhood a day after Mrs. Gandhi’s killing, has been convicted in a case involving the killing of five Sikhs.

YAOUNDE - The government of Cameroon has restored the internet to the country's two English-speaking regions after a three-month blackout, but it is unclear whether the move will be enough to restart dialogue to end the unrest that began in November.Abeng Sylvan moved his roadside cyber cafe to Yaounde from Bamenda when the internet was cut in the northwest. The connection was restored late Thursday."I was almost losing hope," Sylvan said. "Paying my rents and all the light bills was really difficult. … But when I heard it came, I was very happy."But he may have to wait to move his business home.Cameroon government spokesperson Issa Tchiroma Bakary says the internet is being reinstated gradually to the two English-speaking regions, the northwest and the southeast.The internet was cut in January following violent unrest related to an ongoing strike. The government said the strikers were using social media to spread secessionist and anti-government messages.The minister of post and telecommunication, Libom Lili Keng, says although service is back, controls will be intensified.She says the government is equipped to control internet use, and Cameroon's security forces have platforms to track and control people — just as is done in all other countries. She added that Cameroonians should not abuse social media.Restoring the internet is one of three conditions posed by the organizers of the six-month strike before they will resume dialogue with the government.The other conditions are that all people arrested in relation to the strike be released unconditionally, and that the state withdraw the soldiers deployed to English-speaking zones since the start of the strike.

Former President Barack Obama has once again called for increased resources to combat drug abuse in the United States. In a recent speech, Obama emphasized the need for a comprehensive approach to address the ongoing drug crisis in the country.Obama highlighted the importance of prevention, treatment, and enforcement efforts in tackling drug abuse. He emphasized the need for more funding for addiction treatment programs, as well as increased support for law enforcement agencies to crack down on drug trafficking.The former president also stressed the importance of addressing the root causes of drug abuse, including economic instability, mental health issues, and lack of access to quality healthcare. He called for a holistic approach that focuses on both prevention and treatment, rather than solely relying on punitive measures.Obama's comments come at a time when drug overdose deaths continue to rise in the United States, with opioids being a major contributing factor. He urged lawmakers to prioritize funding for drug abuse prevention and treatment programs, and to work towards creating a more comprehensive and effective strategy to combat the drug crisis.As the country continues to grapple with the devastating effects of drug abuse, Obama's call for increased resources and a comprehensive approach to tackling the issue is a timely reminder of the urgent need for action.

The Italian government has stepped up efforts to obtain the release of a journalist from La Repubblica newspaper, who was kidnapped in Afghanistan 11 days ago. Media reports say the Taleban claim to have killed one of two Afghans abducted with the journalist. Sabina Castelfranco reports for VOA from Rome, the kidnappers have said they will grant authorities more time to comply with their conditions.The kidnappers of Italian journalist Daniele Mastrogiacomo had set Friday as the deadline for the release of three Taleban prisoners. This is their key condition for the release of the journalist from La Repubblica newspaper.The Taleban have also requested that Italy withdraw its 1,900 troops based in Afghanistan. On Friday, Taleban spokesman Mullah Ibrahim Hanifi told news media more time would be granted to the authorities to comply with its requests.Hanifi also said the Afghan driver was killed on Thursday. News of the killing further raised concerns in Italy over the fate of the journalist and his Afghan interpreter.The Italian government has increased its efforts to find a way to secure the release of the journalist, who had traveled to southern Afghanistan to interview Taleban leaders. Prime Minister Romano Prodi said he met with visiting Afghan lawmakers and discussed the kidnapping.Foreign Minister Massimo D'Alema said after a Cabinet meeting Friday that authorities have multiplied their efforts to find the path to a solution and to obtain the release of Daniele Mastrogiacomo."Contacts are under way," he said. "This is a complex matter, which requires contacts between different governments and institutions, and cannot be resolved in just a few hours."

In a controversial move, Bosnian Serb authorities have detained a man who was leading protests over the death of his son. The man, whose identity has not been disclosed, has been a vocal advocate for justice following the death of his son in what he claims was a suspicious circumstance.The protests, which have been ongoing for weeks, have drawn attention to the lack of accountability in the region's justice system. Many have criticized the detention of the man as a violation of freedom of speech and peaceful assembly.The man's family and supporters have expressed concern for his well-being while in custody and are calling for his immediate release. The authorities have not provided any information on the charges against him or the reasons for his detention.The situation remains tense as protests continue in support of the detained man and calls for justice for his son. The international community has also expressed concern over the situation and urged Bosnian Serb authorities to respect human rights and due process.This is a developing story and updates will be provided as more information becomes available.

At least 13 people have been killed in northern Venezuela due to severe flooding in the region. Heavy rains have caused rivers to overflow, leading to widespread destruction in several communities.The worst-hit area is the state of Zulia, where numerous homes have been swept away and roads have been washed out. Rescue teams are working tirelessly to evacuate residents and provide assistance to those affected by the disaster.Local authorities have declared a state of emergency in the affected areas and are urging residents to remain vigilant as more rain is expected in the coming days.

Germany has defeated Japan, 2-0, to reach the quarterfinal round of the Women's World Cup football (soccer) tournament in China. VOA's Jim Stevenson was at the game in Hangzhou, where the defending champions seemed to struggle at times, despite playing a relatively weaker opponent.Germany opened the scoring in Hangzhou's Dragon Stadium with a 21st minute strike from captain Birgit Prinz. The score was the 13th career goal for Prinz in a Women's World Cup game, making her the all-time scorer at the event. Her first goal came in the 1995 tournament.The German women ran past the Japanese defenders for most of the game, but came up short on several scoring opportunities. Prinz told VOA sports that fact bothers her, and makes her record somewhat meaningless."Honestly, after the game it is not so important for me. And I was not satisfied with my game today," said Prinz.German coach Silvia Neid was watching anxiously as her team clung to a one-goal lead for most of the game."We did not want to wait for the opponent to score a goal," said Neid. "So we tried to win this game. We tried to scores ourselves. We went for the second one. Especially for the midfield, it was not an easy game since they had to do a lot of running. But we prepared well and it showed on the pitch."

American journalist Roxana Saberi, who was charged with espionage against Iran, has been convicted and condemned to eight years in prison, after being tried behind closed doors. The father of the dual national Saberi, who confirmed the verdict, also says that she was "tricked" into confessing.The Iranian court sentenced American-Iranian journalist Roxana Saberi to eight years in prison.The 31-year-old Saberi, who is a dual American-Iranian citizen, has reported for National Public Radio and the British Broadcasting Corporation and was arrested in January. She was charged with "spying for the United States," and put on trial Monday. The U.S. State Department has rejected the spy charges as "baseless." U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton said Saturday she was disappointed by the sentencing and said the U.S. government would continue to raise concerns to the Iranian government.Saberi's father Reza Saberi confirmed the sentence against his daughter, and argued that she had been "tricked" into confessing to what he called, "bogus charges." Her attorney says that he will appeal.Reza Moini, of Paris-based Reporters Without Borders, says that his organization is protesting what amounts to a "sham" trial and that the charges don't even correspond with Iran's own penal code.Moini says that the condemnation doesn't correspond with the definition of espionage in Iran's penal code, articles 501 and 502, and the charge of espionage doesn't fit Saberi's case, either. But, what's important, he argues, is that the court tried her behind closed doors so that she couldn't defend herself properly.

Thirty-two former U.S. diplomats and 11 aid groups on Monday urged U.S. Secretary of State Mike Pompeo not to eliminate a key State Department refugee bureau, warning that the move would be "an error of grave proportion."In a letter to Pompeo, the former diplomats and national security advisers who served in both Democratic and Republican administrations said eliminating the Bureau of Population, Refugees and Migration (PRM) office would impact the U.S. ability to influence global refugee policy.The letter comes barely a year after 58 U.S. policy experts warned Rex Tillerson, the former secretary of state, against such a move."We believe this would be an error of grave proportion, and we would urge close consultation with the U.S. Congress before such a critically important measure is even considered," the former officials and aid groups wrote.FILE - U.S. Secretary of State Mike Pompeo speaks to reporters at United Nations headquarters, in New York, July 20, 2018.Since taking office in January last year, the Trump administration has cut the number of refugees it admits into the country, introduced stricter vetting rules and quit negotiations on a voluntary pact to deal with global migration."We are convinced that the elimination of PRM's assistance functions would have profound and negative implications for the Secretary of State's capacity to influence policy issues of key concern to the United States," the groups wrote.

A Swiss Red Cross worker who was kidnapped in the southern Philippines has been released after being held hostage for several months. The worker, identified as Andreas Notter, was abducted along with two colleagues in January while visiting a water project in the region.Notter was released on Thursday following negotiations between the Philippine government and the kidnappers, who are believed to be members of the militant group Abu Sayyaf. The other two hostages were freed earlier in the week.The Swiss government has expressed relief over Notter's release and thanked the Philippine authorities for their efforts to secure his freedom. The Red Cross has also welcomed the news, stating that their focus now is on ensuring the well-being of the freed worker.Kidnappings by militant groups, particularly in the southern Philippines, have been a long-standing issue in the region. The Swiss Red Cross has stated that they will continue their humanitarian work in the country despite the risks involved.

A young boy was tragically killed in the recent clashes between Egyptian police and protesters in Cairo. The violence erupted during a demonstration against government corruption and police brutality.The boy, identified as Ahmed Mahmoud, was only 10 years old when he was struck by a stray bullet fired by police officers attempting to disperse the crowd. Witnesses reported seeing the boy fall to the ground as chaos erupted around him.The clashes resulted in several injuries and arrests, as protesters clashed with security forces in the streets of the Egyptian capital.

The German Cyber Agency has recently criticized Yahoo for failing to cooperate in a hacking probe. The agency had requested assistance from the tech company in investigating a cyber attack that targeted a German government agency.Despite repeated requests for information and cooperation, Yahoo has allegedly been unresponsive and uncooperative. This lack of cooperation has frustrated the agency's efforts to track down the hackers and prevent further cyber attacks.In response to Yahoo's lack of assistance, the German Cyber Agency has issued a public statement condemning the company's behavior. They have emphasized the importance of cooperation between tech companies and government agencies in order to combat cyber threats effectively.This incident highlights the ongoing challenges that law enforcement agencies face in investigating cyber crimes and securing sensitive data. It also raises questions about the level of responsibility that tech companies have in assisting with these investigations.Yahoo has yet to respond to the agency's criticisms. It remains to be seen whether they will comply with the agency's requests for assistance in the hacking probe.

CHIPINGE, ZIMBABWE - Here at Chachacha village in southeast Zimbabwe, men force their cattle into a truck bound for market.Drought brought on by particularly severe El Nino weather patterns has left 14 million people across Zimbabwe and southern Africa without enough to eat. The current rainy season has been the driest in 35 years, according to the U.N. World Food Program.Johannes Chibvongodze is among the people selling his cattle because he can't afford to feed them, or buy food for himself and his family. Low rainfall has reduced grazing land.Chibvongodze says jobs are hard to come by, and families are competing with others coming over from Mozambique.Johannes Chibvongodze of Chipinge, Zimbabwe, says he sold his cattle because he can’t afford to feed them. Low rainfall has reduced grazing land, and the sale helped raise money to feed his family, March 2016. (S. Mhofu/VOA)While this year's rains finally came, it was too late to save many crops from the drought.“For me to plant anything new now,” Chibvongodze said, is not “a workable solution.”The capital of Harare is about 600 kilometers away, and locals say government assistance has been slow to arrive.The U.N.'s Food and Agriculture Organization has been supplying herders in Chipinge district with feed at reduced prices to help them fatten their cattle.And some farmers in Chipinge are busy planting, although the Zimbabwe meteorological services department announced last week that the already-shortened rainy season will end in mid-April.But the question of what to plant is a tough one."We would rather encourage farmers to go for small grains — especially the finger millet, pearl millet and sorghum — because they are a bit tolerant in terms of drought,” said Taringana Makiwa of the state-owned Agricultural Extension Service, which advises Zimbabweans on farming. “Most of our farmers used to go for maize crop.

After days of intense negotiations, delegates at the United Nations Climate Change Conference in Glasgow have finally reached a historic agreement. The deal, known as the Glasgow Climate Pact, aims to accelerate action on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and limiting global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius. Key points of the agreement include commitments to phase out coal use, increase financing for developing countries to cope with the impacts of climate change, and establish a mechanism for countries to regularly report on their progress towards meeting their emissions reduction targets. UN Secretary-General António Guterres praised the agreement as a "significant step forward" but also warned that much more work is needed to meet the goals of the Paris Agreement. The Glasgow Climate Pact will now be formally adopted by all 197 countries participating in the conference.

WASHINGTON - Despite last month’s suspected attack on the U.S. State Department’s email system and website, there’s "no reason to believe classified information was compromised," a spokesman said.The department had revealed that a portion of its email system and public website were shut down over the weekend – weeks after authorities detected suspicious activity believed to be the work of an outside hacker."The State Department, like any other large organization that has a global span, is a constant target of cyberattacks," spokesman Jeff Rathke told reporters Monday at a routine daily briefing.He said the department, after detecting "activity of concern several weeks ago," developed a response with cybersecurity experts from the Department of Homeland Security and other agencies and is "implementing carefully planned improvements to the security of our main unclassified network."Rathke, in explaining why parts of the email system weren’t disconnected until this weekend, said the action was part of previously planned maintenance.He said he had no information to share on the source of the attack, which remains under investigation.The intrusion happened about the same time the White House announced several of its unclassified computer systems were hacked into by an outside intruder. In that case, officials were quick to lay blame on Russian hackers, possibly working in cooperation with the Kremlin.Cyber-attacks on U.S. and Western governmental and military assets have been on the uptick lately.Just in the last few weeks, Chinese hackers reportedly dug into 800,000 employee records at the U.S.

In a recent speech, former President George W. Bush encouraged Muslim moderates to speak out against extremism and promote tolerance and understanding. Bush emphasized the importance of countering the narrative of radical groups who distort the true teachings of Islam. He also praised the efforts of moderate Muslims who are working towards peace and unity in their communities. Bush's message comes at a critical time as the world continues to face the threat of extremism and violence. By highlighting the role of moderate voices, Bush hopes to inspire positive change and foster greater dialogue among different religious groups.

Austria's interim government ministers were sworn in on Friday following the resignation of the previous administration due to a video scandal. The video scandal, which was released earlier this week, showed the former vice chancellor discussing potential deals with a woman posing as a Russian investor.The scandal prompted the resignation of Vice Chancellor Heinz-Christian Strache and led to the collapse of the coalition government between the conservative People's Party and the far-right Freedom Party. In response to the crisis, President Alexander Van der Bellen appointed a group of non-partisan experts to lead the government until snap elections can be held in September.The interim ministers, who were sworn in at the presidential palace in Vienna, are tasked with overseeing the day-to-day operations of the government and preparing for the upcoming elections. The new government faces the challenge of restoring public trust in the political system following the scandal, which has sparked widespread outrage and calls for reform.The video scandal has raised concerns about corruption and the influence of foreign actors in Austrian politics. Many citizens are demanding accountability and transparency from their leaders, as they look towards the upcoming elections as an opportunity for change.

Many are expressing skepticism over the recent cease-fire agreement in Syria, with concerns about whether all parties involved will adhere to the terms of the truce. The agreement, brokered by Russia and Turkey, aims to halt the violence in the war-torn country and allow for humanitarian aid to reach those in need.However, previous cease-fire attempts in Syria have been short-lived and often violated by various factions fighting in the conflict. With multiple armed groups and foreign powers involved in the war, the complexity of the situation makes it difficult to guarantee a lasting peace.Some analysts are hopeful that this latest cease-fire could be a step towards a more permanent resolution to the conflict, while others remain cautious given the track record of previous agreements falling apart. The United Nations has urged all parties to respect the cease-fire and work towards a political solution to end the years of bloodshed in Syria.As the world watches closely to see if the cease-fire will hold, the fate of millions of Syrians caught in the crossfire hangs in the balance. Only time will tell if this agreement will bring much-needed relief to the war-torn country.

Organizers of the vote by Iraqi expatriates say some two-thirds of eligable Iraqis living abroad have participated in the historic election so far.The International Organization for Migration said Sunday more than 186,000 Iraqis voted Friday and Saturday in the Middle East, North America, Australia and Western Europe.The three-day process runs until the end of Sunday across 36 cities in 14 nations, including Australia, Britain, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, Iran, Jordan, the Netherlands, Sweden, Syria, Turkey, United Arab Emirates and the United States.Some information for this report provided by AP, AFP.

BANGUI, CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC - The U.N. Food and Agriculture Organization says the Central African Republic needs to find more work for young people if it wants to avoid further violence. The agency is working to show how gardening can be career opportunity for youth involved with armed groups.In a village 26 kilometers north of Bangui, drummers welcome FAO workers who have come to see how a group here is working to grow water melons and other produce.Villagers say until recently gardener Levy Koulbe was the head of a group of anti-balaka, a militia from mainly Christian communities. The anti-balaka started out as self-defense groups, but increasingly are regarded as bandits.Koulbe said he is one of the anti-balaka who came from the bush, and when they got to Bangui no one looked after them, so they suffered. He said he is grateful now that one of his relatives, Louis Koro, advised them to join this farmers’ group and learn gardening. Koro also is president of this gardeners’ group.As for payment, Koulbe said they were told they might each get about $6.50 a day, but so far they are not being paid that. Regardless, he said he is glad to be learning gardening as he thinks it will be profitable for him in future.Koro confirms there is not much pay. "To say the workers are paid would be an overstatement.

WASHINGTON - Islamic State (IS) militants pledged to fight to the death after U.S.-backed Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) announced the start of the final push to liberate the last IS pockets in the eastern countryside of Deir el-Zour.Suhaib Jaber, a Syrian journalist from Deir el-Zour working with the Euphrates Post, told VOA that IS urged its members, as well as civilians in the areas under its control, to unite and fight without negotiations or surrender.“Disputes escalated between IS Syrian fighters and IS foreign fighters after the disappearance of their leader, Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi. The allegiance came to unite them in one battle,” Jaber said.IS has resorted to the “Allegiance of Death” tactic during its last hours in the areas under its control after being cornered during battles to free Mosul, Iraq, and Raqqa, Syria.The operation against the last IS pockets in Deir el-Zour, code named Al Jazeera Storm, was launched Sept. 9, 2017, and was stalled for months at the beginning of 2018 after SDF fighters shifted attention to fight the Turkish military incursion against the Kurdish-held area of Afrin, in north Syria.The operation to liberate the last IS pockets in Deir el-Zour resumed May 1, 2018, as Kurdish fighters returned from the Afrin area.“

BUENOS AIRES - An Argentine court sentenced former President Carlos Menem on Tuesday to four-and-a-half years in prison for embezzlement. The official judicial news agency said that the local court also sentenced former Economy Minister Domingo Cavallo to three years and six months for his role in illegal payments to staffers authorized by Menem during his 1989-1999 presidency. Investigators said Menem and Cavallo were part of a scheme to overpay officials and later split the take among all of those involved. The overpayments were intended for security and intelligence expenses. Menem is currently a senator representing La Rioja province where he was born. That status as a lawmaker protects him from being imprisoned. The 85-year-old was absent from the proceedings citing health problems. The court also sentenced former Justice Minister Raul Granillo Ocampo to three years and three months in prison, while former Environment Secretariat Maria Julia Alsogaray was absolved. She also faces other corruption charges and is under house arrest.

The Islamic State (IS) has declared its intention to fight to the death in the battle for control of areas near Deir el-Zour in Syria. The group, known for its brutal tactics and extremist ideology, has been actively engaging in clashes with Syrian government forces and their allies in the region.IS fighters have been putting up a fierce resistance, using guerrilla tactics and suicide attacks to hold onto their remaining territory in the area. The battle for Deir el-Zour has been ongoing for several years, with both sides suffering significant casualties.The loss of Deir el-Zour would be a major blow to IS, as the region is strategically important and serves as a key supply route for the group. However, the militants have shown a willingness to continue fighting despite facing overwhelming odds.The international community has been closely monitoring the situation in Deir el-Zour, with many expressing concern about the humanitarian impact of the ongoing conflict. Aid organizations have called for safe passage for civilians trapped in the area, as access to food, water, and medical supplies remains limited.As the battle rages on, the fate of Deir el-Zour hangs in the balance, with both sides preparing for a final showdown that could have far-reaching consequences for the region.

Burma's de facto leader, Aung San Suu Kyi, had to leave a political rally in Naypyidaw after falling ill. The Nobel Peace Prize laureate was seen coughing and struggling to speak during her speech at the rally.Suu Kyi's spokesperson stated that she was suffering from exhaustion and was advised by her doctors to rest. This news comes amidst a tense political climate in Burma, with the military still holding considerable power despite Suu Kyi's civilian government being in place.Suu Kyi has been a prominent figure in Burma's politics for decades, advocating for democracy and human rights in the country. Her illness has raised concerns among her supporters, but her spokesperson reassured the public that she is expected to make a full recovery soon.The incident has caused speculation about Suu Kyi's health and ability to lead the country effectively. However, for now, she remains a central figure in Burma's political landscape, despite this setback.

Thousands of residents in Arizona have been forced to evacuate as a massive wildfire threatens the city. The wildfire, which has been burning out of control for days, has already destroyed several homes and businesses in its path.Emergency responders are working tirelessly to contain the blaze and protect the remaining structures in the area. However, the high winds and dry conditions have made it difficult to control the spread of the fire.Evacuation orders have been issued for several neighborhoods, and residents have been urged to seek shelter in designated evacuation centers. Firefighters are also conducting door-to-door checks to ensure that everyone has safely evacuated the area.The Governor of Arizona has declared a state of emergency and requested additional resources from neighboring states to help fight the wildfire. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.Authorities are asking residents to stay vigilant and follow evacuation orders to ensure their safety. Our thoughts are with those affected by this devastating wildfire, and we hope for a swift containment of the blaze.

A United Nations expert has called on North Korea to allow "unimpeded access" to aid organizations in order to help combat the spread of the virus in the country. The expert highlighted the importance of providing assistance to North Korea to prevent a potential humanitarian crisis. It is crucial for the government of North Korea to cooperate with international organizations and allow them access to the country to provide much-needed support in the fight against the virus. The UN expert emphasized the need for transparency and cooperation in order to effectively combat the pandemic in North Korea.

WASHINGTON - Ousted National Security Adviser Michael Flynn will not honor a subpoena issued by a Senate committee looking into Russia's meddling in the 2016 presidential election, the panel's chairman said Thursday.Republican Sen. Richard Burr of North Carolina told reporters that Flynn's lawyer has informed the panel he will not abide by a subpoena for private documents. The decision comes as no surprise, legal experts say, given that Flynn would be waiving his constitutional protection against self-incrimination by turning over personal documents to the committee. Flynn, though his lawyer, had earlier asked for immunity from "unfair prosecution" in exchange for agreeing to cooperate with the committee. "We'll figure out on Gen. Flynn what the next step if any is,'" Burr said. The Senate committee is one of several on Capitol Hill investigating possible collusion between Russia and President Donald Trump's 2016 campaign. Flynn is also the target of other congressional investigations as well as an ongoing FBI counterintelligence probe and a grand jury inquiry. Flynn was fired from his position as Trump's national security adviser in February. At the time, Trump said he fired Flynn because he misled senior administration officials, including the vice president, about his contacts with Russian officials.

Israeli security guards at Ben Gurion Airport successfully thwarted a hijack attempt on a flight bound for Tel Aviv today. The incident occurred as passengers were boarding the plane, when a group of individuals attempted to force their way into the cockpit.The quick response of the security guards prevented the hijackers from gaining control of the aircraft. The guards were able to subdue the individuals and detain them until local authorities arrived on the scene.Authorities have not yet released any information about the identities or motives of the hijackers. The passengers on the flight were unharmed during the incident and the plane was able to depart on schedule after a thorough security check.The Israeli Security Agency has commended the guards for their swift and decisive actions in preventing a potentially dangerous situation. They have assured the public that they are taking all necessary precautions to ensure the safety and security of all passengers traveling to and from Ben Gurion Airport.

Undersecretary of State Nicholas Burns has called for new sanctions against Iran because of its ongoing nuclear program. The call was made during a visit to the Turkish capital Ankara, where Burns met with Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Turkey, which borders on Iran, is viewed by the United States as a key strategic ally in the region. Dorian Jones reports for the VOA from Istanbul. U.S. Undersecretary of State Nicholas Burns called for members of the United Nations Security Council to implement a third round of sanctions against Iran because of its contested nuclear program. Speaking after talks with Turkish Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdogan, Undersecretary Burns said the United States has the strong backing of France and the United Kingdom and is seeking support from Russia and China.Burns said the U.S. is committed to a peaceful resolution of the dispute with Iran. He warned that sanctions can only succeed if fully implemented. "If diplomacy is to be effective, we will need the support of all countries in Asia, in the Middle East , in Europe, to contribute to the sanctions against Iran," Burns said.Washington says Iran is secretly building nuclear weapons, a charge that Tehran denies.

A senior United Nations official has expressed disappointment in the Soviet invasion of Georgia during the Olympic Games in Beijing but says sports can be a powerful force in promoting peace and development. VOA's Scott Bobb reports from the Chinese capital.The Special Advisor on Sport (for Development and Peace), to the U.N. Secretary-General Wilfried Lemke, says the Olympic community was saddened by the outbreak of fighting in the separatist Georgian enclave, Ossetia, on the opening day of the Olympic Games."We feel it and we are very disappointed because that makes a shadow on the Olympics, definitely," he said.Lemke told reporters Friday in Beijing that although sports does not have the physical power to stop war it can help prevent it by building bridges between communities and encouraging peace and development.He said athletes can act as role models and noted that many international sports figures work with U.N. agencies to promote public awareness of various social issues.U.N. officials say China has made great efforts to emphasize environmental sustainability at the Olympics by reducing air pollution and promoting public transportation. And they say it has promoted public awareness of other issues such as health, poverty alleviation, gender equality and HIV/AIDS.The head of the World Olympians Association, 1968 high-jump gold medalist Richard Fosbury, said the Olympic spirit has also influenced policies of the Chinese government."

In a surprising turn of events, Chinese tech executive Meng Wanzhou was released on bail in Canada while US President Donald Trump suggested the possibility of a trade deal with China. Meng, the CFO of Huawei Technologies, was arrested earlier this month in Vancouver at the request of US authorities for allegedly violating sanctions against Iran.Trump's comments about a potential trade agreement come amid escalating tensions between the US and China over trade and technology. The arrest of Meng, who is the daughter of Huawei's founder, has further strained relations between the two economic powerhouses.Despite the ongoing legal battle facing Meng, Trump's willingness to consider a trade deal with China could signal a shift in the tense relations between the two countries. However, details of any potential agreement remain unclear.As Meng awaits her next court appearance, the world watches anxiously to see how this high-profile case will impact US-China relations and the global tech industry.
